name: OpenTerminalUI
reason: Verified against the live repo: MIT LICENSE file is real ("Copyright (c) 2026 OpenTerminalUI Contributors"), and it is active (last commit 2026-07-11, 51 stars). The interaction-design claims are backed by real code, not just README marketing — frontend/src/components/layout/ contains actual CommandBar.tsx (GO Bar/Ctrl+G), CommandPalette.tsx (Ctrl+K), commanding.ts, SplitPane.tsx, and LaunchpadGrid/Panels/Workspace.tsx. This is the strongest interaction-design match found: it implements exactly the entity+function grammar vc-brain wants. NOT "adopt" because the frontend is React 18 + TypeScript + Vite + Tailwind + Zustand + React Router with a mandatory build step (package.json, vite.config.ts confirmed) — fundamentally incompatible with vc-brain's zero-dependency no-build vanilla SPA, so nothing runs as-is. It IS worth adapting: commanding.ts (~750 lines) is a self-contained grammar parser (35+ function codes, aliases, regex tokenization, lookup maps, a portable fuzzyScore()) with near-zero React coupling that can be translated to vanilla JS relatively cleanly, and the pane/workspace/shell components are strong structural references. No network graph exists (confirmed absent), so vc-brain's graph-viz need is unmet here.
key_files_or_ideas: Crown jewel to translate: frontend/src/components/layout/commanding.ts (~750 lines) — entity+function command-grammar parser ("AAPL DES"), 35+ function codes with aliases, regex tokenization + lookup maps, and a self-contained fuzzyScore() with minimal React deps; ports cleanly to vanilla JS for vc-brain's command bar and universal fuzzy search. Reference-grade UI (React/Zustand-coupled, reimplement not copy): frontend/src/components/layout/CommandBar.tsx (GO Bar / Ctrl+G), CommandPalette.tsx (~350 lines, Ctrl+K, reuses fuzzyScore), SplitPane.tsx + LaunchpadGrid.tsx + LaunchpadPanels.tsx + LaunchpadWorkspace.tsx (tiled panes / saveable workspace), TerminalShell.tsx, PanelChrome.tsx, HudOverlay.tsx. Also useful as design spec: 8-tab entity page pattern (pages/ folder) and F1-F9 workspace hotkeys. Gap: no network-graph component exists — vc-brain must source graph viz elsewhere.

name: gloomberb
reason: Verified against the live repo. Its Bloomberg-style command grammar is exactly vc-brain's target and worth studying closely: entity+function mnemonics (DES AAPL, GP NVDA, TOP, backtick quick-ticker, SRCH) organized by category (research FA/HP/SEC, charting GP/GIP/CMP, markets MOST/HM/ECON), Ctrl+P command mode, vi nav (j/k, h/l), Tab-cycled panes, and a plugin registry that adds panes/tabs/columns/commands/providers — a strong model for vc-brain's function registry and linked tiled panes. BUT no frontend code is liftable. package.json confirms the UI is React 19 rendering through @opentui/react + @opentui/core (a terminal-cell layout engine, NOT the browser DOM), wrapped by electrobun for the desktop build. This is a double mismatch with vc-brain's zero-dependency vanilla-JS DOM SPA: wrong language (React), wrong render target (terminal cells, not DOM), and a required Bun build step (bun run scripts/build.ts + electrobun build). The candidate's own 'design reference, not liftable code' caveat is correct and actually understated. Verdict: study and re-implement the grammar/registry/keymap in vanilla JS; do not port files.
license_confirmed: MIT — real LICENSE file, Copyright (c) 2026 Gloomberb Contributors. Adds only a non-binding attribution request ("appreciated and encouraged, though not legally required beyond preserving the above copyright notice"); otherwise standard permissive MIT, fully compatible.
key_files_or_ideas: Design ideas to mine (re-implement in vanilla JS, do not port): (1) command grammar = <FUNCTION> [ENTITY] mnemonics grouped by category — research/charting/markets verbs like DES, GP, TOP, FA, HP, SEC, SRCH, plus backtick quick-ticker lookup; (2) function/command registry as the extensibility spine (plugins register panes, tabs, columns, command-bar commands, status-bar widgets, data providers); (3) keyboard model — Ctrl+P command palette, vi nav j/k + h/l, Tab to cycle panes; (4) src/ layout worth reading for structure: src/core (command parsing), src/plugins + PLUGINS.md (registry contract), src/renderers + src/components (pane composition), src/react/input.ts (keybinding/input handling). Repo is github.com/vincelwt/gloomberb; PLUGINS.md documents the registration API.

name: jmrothberg/bloomberg-terminal
reason: Verified against the live repo (not marketing). Genuinely zero-build: one index.html, 576KB, 11,352 lines = single <style> (~1,211 lines) + single <script> (~9,690 lines JS, 437 fns). Only external asset is a Google Fonts link (IBM Plex Mono), trivially self-hosted. Active: created 2026-04-21, last commit 2026-06-25 (v1.7.11), but 1 star / 0 forks = unvetted personal project. LICENSE NUANCE: 'MIT' appears ONLY in README prose ('MIT — do whatever you want with this code'); there is NO LICENSE file (raw 404, contents API confirms absent, GitHub license.spdx_id=null). Legally a valid MIT grant, but not tool-detectable/clean. Reusable, technically separable frontend assets that map onto vc-brain: (1) CSS skin-variable system (--bg-panel/--text-primary/--color-positive|negative/--highlight/--stale) mirroring the skeleton+skin approach; (2) tiled-pane grid + pull-down tray interaction (.panel/.panel-title/.panel-tray/.panel-caret + tray-open, grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr, chip navigation); (3) inline-SVG micro-charts renderSparkline (L6285), squarifiedTreemap (L6095)+heatColor (L6160)+renderHeatMap (L6009). NOT usable as-is: JS is bigger than the marketed 6.8k and ~90% is irrelevant domain feeds (44 CORS-proxy + 80 Yahoo refs, plus Polymarket/flights/AIS vessels/seismic/storms/news-RSS/Fidelity-CSV); no fuzzy command palette and no network/graph viz (two of vc-brain's headline wants); entity model (persons/forums/evidence/briefs) entirely absent. Verdict: port the pane-grid/tray CSS and the sparkline/treemap SVG functions and study the command-input+chip pattern — adapt, not adopt.
license_confirmed: MIT stated only in README prose ('MIT — do whatever you want with this code. But please don't claim you're affiliated with Bloomberg L.P.'). NO LICENSE file in repo (raw LICENSE 404; contents API confirms absent; GitHub API license.spdx_id=null). Valid MIT grant by author intent, but not a clean/tool-detectable license file.
key_files_or_ideas: Single file: index.html (576KB, 11,352 lines). Reusable pieces by location: CSS skin variables in the <style> block (--bg-panel/--bg-primary/--bg-tray/--border-color/--text-primary/--text-secondary/--text-dim/--text-accent/--color-positive/--color-negative/--color-neutral/--highlight/--stale); tiled-pane grid + pull-down tray CSS around L112-162 (.panel, .panel-title, .panel-tray, .panel-caret, .panel-content, .tray-open, grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr); SVG micro-chart functions renderSparkline (L6285), squarifiedTreemap (L6095), heatColor (L6160), renderHeatMap (L6009), renderChart (L6806). Ideas: panel-title chip navigation to reassign/configure panes; keyboard UX (R/H/ESC/Enter, up/down autocomplete). Ignore the entire data layer (Yahoo/CoinGecko/Polymarket/flights/vessels/seismic/storms/news-RSS/Fidelity-CSV import) — irrelevant to vc-brain.

name: feremabraz/bloomberg-terminal
reason: Verified against the live repo, not the marketing. License is genuinely MIT (confirmed in repo sidebar + README) — no legal blocker. But two hard facts kill any code lift into our zero-dependency vanilla-JS static SPA: (1) It is a full Next.js 15 App Router + React 19 project requiring pnpm, Node LTS, and a .env with AlphaVantage/OpenAI/Upstash-Redis keys — every component is a `"use client"` .tsx using React hooks, Jotai atoms, React Query, and shadcn/ui. There is no build-free artifact and nothing separable; the frontend is inseparable from React/Jotai. (2) The headline selling points are overstated. I opened the actual command/keyboard code: `components/bloomberg/core/keyboard-shortcuts.tsx` is a ~120-line Ctrl/Alt/Shift key-combo handler plus a help modal — NO command grammar, NO GO/TICK verbs, NO fuzzy matching, NO palette. The only "command-ish" surface, `components/bloomberg/layout/terminal-filter-bar.tsx`, is a view-toggle filter bar (checkboxes + dropdowns bound to Jotai atoms), not a command line. So the exact interactions we most want (entity+function command grammar, universal fuzzy search) are not even implemented here to port. Also no network-graph/entity-graph and no fuzzy search anywhere. Activity is thinner than claimed: ~14 commits total, real development in May–June 2025, with a single March 5, 2026 commit that is just a React RSC CVE security-patch merge — not ongoing feature work. What remains genuinely useful is DESIGN reference only: the terminal chrome/layout decomposition (terminal-header, terminal-layout, terminal-filter-bar, keyboard-shortcuts help modal, confirmation-modal, shortcut-indicator) and the Bloomberg-style dark visual language via Tailwind — patterns to study and re-implement, not code to lift. Given our zero-dep/no-build constraint and that the marquee command-bar+fuzzy-search behavior isn't actually in the code, this is study-the-design, not adopt or port.
key_files_or_ideas: Worth STUDYING (design only, re-implement in vanilla JS): components/bloomberg/layout/ (bloomberg-terminal.tsx, terminal-header.tsx, terminal-layout.tsx, terminal-filter-bar.tsx) for terminal chrome + view decomposition; components/bloomberg/core/keyboard-shortcuts.tsx for a keyboard-shortcut registry + help-modal UX (Ctrl/Alt/Shift combos, Alt+D dark toggle); core/confirmation-modal.tsx and shortcut-indicator.tsx for terminal-style affordances; Tailwind bloombergColors theme for the dark Bloomberg palette. NOT present / do NOT expect to find: a real command-grammar palette, fuzzy search, GO/TICK verb parser, or any network/entity graph — the README and third-party blogs claim fuzzy-matching command palette with GO/TICK, but the repo code does not implement it. Repo facts: Next.js 15 App Router, React 19, TS 98.1%, Jotai + React Query + shadcn/ui + Motion + Biome, Upstash Redis + AlphaVantage + OpenAI; requires pnpm build + .env keys; ~14 commits; last commit 2026-03-05 (RSC CVE patch), prior dev May–Jun 2025; ~1.5k stars, 248 forks; MIT.

name: Neuberg
reason: Two independent hard blockers to any code reuse, leaving only design value. (1) License: BSL 1.1 (Bauhinia AI Limited), non-commercial-only with a PERPETUAL Change Date (never converts to open source) — not OSI-open; commercial use in a product needs a separate license. (2) Stack incompatibility: React 19 + Vite + Express 5 backend + WebSocket, requires npm build — fundamentally incompatible with the zero-dependency vanilla-JS no-build SPA; adopting anything would mean a full React->vanilla rewrite. It is also trading/market-data oriented (Alpaca/Hyperliquid/Polymarket), which vc-brain explicitly does not need, and has no command-bar grammar, fuzzy search, entity pages, or network graph — the core vc-brain requirements. What remains worth studying is purely design: the drag-drop tiled/linked-pane composition and the 516-panel widget catalog as inspiration for the tiled-pane layout goal.
license_confirmed: BSL 1.1 (Business Source License 1.1), licensor Bauhinia AI Limited. Additional Use Grant: non-commercial use only ("may not use the Licensed Work for a Commercial Purpose"). Change Date: none / perpetual (never converts to an open license). NOT OSI open source. Commercial use requires a separate license from the author.
key_files_or_ideas: Frontend separable as a directory at /client (src/, vite.config.ts, index.html, package.json) but React/TSX + Vite build-step, not drop-in. Design references worth studying only: the drag-drop tiled/linked-pane dashboard composition and the 516-panel widget catalog (equities/FX/macro panels). No command-bar grammar, no universal fuzzy search, no entity pages, no network-graph code. Confirmed via github.com/KoNananachan/Neuberg and its LICENSE file; v1.0.0 released 2026-03-31, 326 commits, 120 stars.

name: reconurge/flowsint
reason: Verified live: Apache-2.0 (GitHub API license.spdx_id), actively maintained (pushed_at 2026-07-01, ~18 days ago; 871 commits; 7.4k stars; not archived; ~64MB repo). Conceptually the closest match to vc-brain's entity-graph/network-graph + entity-linking need (persons/forums/evidence as nodes/edges, enrichers, graph exploration UX). BUT the frontend (flowsint-app) is a React 19 + Vite + TypeScript app with a heavy dependency tree (Radix UI, TanStack Query/Router) and a MANDATORY build step (vite build -> dist, served via nginx, proxying to FastAPI/Neo4j/Postgres/Redis). This is squarely incompatible with vc-brain's zero-dependency, no-build, vanilla-JS constraint. Its graph rendering is React-only (@xyflow/react a.k.a. React Flow ^12.6.4 + react-force-graph-2d ^1.27.1, with d3/d3-force + dagre for layout) -- note the candidate write-up's claim that the viz library 'isn't named in docs' is false; they're in package.json. Porting that React/XYFlow code into a vanilla SPA is a rewrite, not a translation, so it does not qualify as 'adapt' at the code level. It also has NO command-bar/entity-function grammar (its own write-up concedes 'Not a command-bar terminal'). The 'thousands of nodes without lag' line is an unverified README claim. Value is limited to studying its node/edge data model, enricher pattern, and d3-force-based graph interaction design -- design/spec only.
key_files_or_ideas: flowsint-app/ (React 19 + Vite + TS frontend, build-required). Ideas worth studying only: node/edge entity-relationship schema (flowsint-types / Pydantic + neo4j-migrations Cypher), the enricher pattern (auto-expanding a node's relationships), and the graph-interaction model rendered via @xyflow/react + react-force-graph-2d with d3-force/dagre layout. None of the frontend code is liftable into a zero-dep vanilla SPA.

name: ninja-keys
reason: MIT license verified (GitHub API spdx_id "MIT" + LICENSE file), so legally usable. But the "maintained" claim is FALSE: last commit to main is 2022-07-01; pushed_at 2024 reflects unmerged PR branches, no GitHub releases, 32 open issues — main frozen ~4 years. It is NOT zero-dependency: package.json (v1.2.2) declares three runtime deps — lit@2.2.6, hotkeys-js@3.8.7, @material/mwc-icon@0.25.3 (candidate note undersold this as just "Lit, small"). The dist file uses bare ESM imports and is NOT a self-contained bundle; "no build step" only works via a CDN module-rewriting URL (unpkg?module) that pulls the full Lit runtime + hotkeys + material icons over the network — which breaks vc-brain's pure-vanilla no-runtime-lib constraint. Functionally it is a single-input Notion/Slack palette with nested actions and plain substring filtering (not real fuzzy, not multi-token grammar); it cannot parse an "AAPL Equity DES" entity+function command line without net-new work. The Lit/TS component code is a rewrite (not a port) to run in vanilla JS, and there is no reusable search/parse core worth extracting. Value is purely the interaction design — keyboard nav, nested-action model, inline hotkey hints, and ::part() theming (which maps well onto vc-brain's skin system). Study the UX, don't take the code.
key_files_or_ideas: Ideas worth studying (not code to copy): (1) command-palette UX — single overlay, keyboard-driven up/down/enter nav, ESC to close, configurable open hotkey; (2) nested-action / submenu model for grouping commands (maps to vc-brain function menus); (3) inline hotkey-hint rendering per row; (4) ::part() pseudo-element theming pattern — clean fit for vc-brain's skeleton+skin CSS since it exposes styling hooks without leaking internals. What to skip: the Lit-based rendering, reactive-property machinery, hotkeys-js/mwc-icon deps, and its substring-only filter (vc-brain needs real fuzzy + entity+function grammar, which ninja-keys does not provide). Repo: github.com/ssleptsov/ninja-keys; relevant files src/ninja-keys.ts, src/ninja-header.ts, src/ninja-action.ts.

name: cmdk
reason: Verified against the live repo. License MIT (GitHub + npm), active (v1.1.1, Mar 2025, 12.8k stars). The full command-menu component is React-only: index.tsx (~1090 lines) hard-depends on React 18 hooks + Radix Dialog and builds via tsup/pnpm, so it is NOT drop-in for a zero-dep no-build vanilla SPA (rules out 'adopt'). However, the fuzzy scorer command-score.ts (~140 lines) is a pure zero-import function (str,str,arr)->number using only ES6 — portable near-verbatim into a plain .js file by stripping the export/TS types. Its weight tuning (contiguous 1.0, word-boundary 0.9/0.8, gap x0.999, distance x0.9, case penalty) is exactly what vc-brain's universal search needs. So we port that one file (adapt) and treat the keyboard-nav + group-reorder-by-best-item logic in index.tsx as design reference. The 'tens of millions of downloads' marketing reflects shadcn/ui rewrapping, not direct relevance.
key_files_or_ideas: PORT: cmdk/src/command-score.ts (~140 lines, pure ES6, zero deps, MIT) — strip export/TS types into a vanilla .js fuzzy matcher for universal search; its scoring weights (contiguous 1.0, space/hyphen boundary 0.9, special-char boundary 0.8, per-skip gap penalty x0.999, distance-from-word-start x0.9, case-mismatch penalty) are the reference-grade piece. REFERENCE ONLY (do not copy — React/Radix-coupled): cmdk/src/index.tsx keyboard navigation model, and the 'reorder groups by their highest-scoring item then sort items by score' filtering pipeline. Skip the component itself — needs React 18 + Radix + a build step.

name: kbar
reason: MIT-licensed (safe to study/reimplement) and actively maintained (v0.1.0-beta.48, ~mid-2025; open issues into late 2025), but technically INCOMPATIBLE with vc-brain's zero-dependency, no-build, vanilla-JS constraint. Verified against the real repo: hard React peer dependency (react ^16-19 + react-dom), Radix react-portal, and react-virtual; a mandatory TypeScript->lib build step; ships as an npm package with no framework-agnostic core and no build-free CDN/UMD path that avoids pulling in React. Adopting or 'porting' it is a full rewrite, not a translation, so it is not liftable. Also corrected a marketing inaccuracy: the pitch claims 'match-sorter' ranking, but the package actually uses fuse.js plus an internal custom matcher (no match-sorter dependency). The real value is the DESIGN: the action-registry model (app declaratively registers {id,name,shortcut,keywords,section,perform,parent} and the bar builds/searches/renders itself) maps directly onto vc-brain's entity+function command grammar and universal search over 10k entities, and its nested-action navigation, keyboard model, and priority-then-fuzzy ordering are worth reimplementing in vanilla JS. Reference, not adopt/adapt.
key_files_or_ideas: Design ideas to borrow (not code): (1) Action registry architecture — declarative actions with {id, name, shortcut, keywords, section, perform, parent} that the palette self-assembles from; maps to vc-brain function grammar (DES/GRAPH/EVIDENCE etc.). (2) useRegisterActions/KBarProvider pattern — a central store where any pane can register/deregister actions dynamically. (3) Nested/child actions (parent field) for drill-down command trees (entity -> functions). (4) Priority-then-fuzzy result ordering with sectioned results and keyboard-first navigation. (5) Search + list virtualization concept for scaling to 10k+ entities. Real deps observed (React-only, do NOT lift): react/react-dom peer, @radix-ui/react-portal, react-virtual, fuse.js, fast-equals, tiny-invariant. Verified: MIT license, TS->lib + webpack build required.

name: react-mosaic
reason: Fully verified, not unverifiable. License is Apache-2.0 (confirmed from the LICENSE file; copyright Kevin Verdieck, originally Palantir) — permissive, no legal barrier. Repo is very active: v7.0.0 released 2026-07-13 (latest commit same day), 4.8k stars, so "established/widely used" is accurate. HOWEVER it is a React 19 + TypeScript component with hard runtime deps (react-dnd + HTML5/touch backends, lodash-es, immutability-helper, uuid) and a mandatory build step (tsup/vite + Less->CSS, Node>=22); it ships NO standalone vanilla/UMD bundle. That directly violates vc-brain's zero-dependency, no-build, vanilla-JS constraint, so it cannot be adopted, and its core UX (drag/drop/resize) is entirely react-dnd-bound — not liftable into vanilla without a full reimplementation. The transferable value is purely the design: the layout-as-a-tree data model and its split/expand/replace/remove + path-traversal API, which is the canonical spec to reimplement for vc-brain's tiled, linked, rearrangeable panes. Verdict is capped at reference by the technical constraint, not the license.
license_confirmed: Apache-2.0 (verified from LICENSE file; copyright Kevin Verdieck, originally developed at Palantir Technologies)
key_files_or_ideas: Reusable as SPEC, not code: (1) the n-ary MosaicNode layout tree (leaves = pane IDs, internal nodes = {direction: 'row'|'column', first, second, splitPercentage}) as the persisted, serializable layout data model for tiled/linked panes; (2) the mutation/traversal API to reimplement in vanilla — getNodeAtPath, getPathToCorner, updateTree, getOtherDirection, createBalancedTreeFromLeaves, and split/expand-to-percentage/replace/remove operations addressed by tree path; (3) interaction patterns to port from scratch (drag-to-rearrange, drag-to-resize splitters, tab groups) since react-mosaic's implementations are react-dnd-bound. Do NOT plan to consume react-mosaic-component from npm — it needs React + a bundler and ships no build-free artifact.

name: FINOS Perspective
reason:
Claims verified and mostly accurate, but wrong tool for vc-brain. License Apache-2.0 (confirmed), genuinely active (v4.5.2 released 2026-07-09, ~11k stars), and there IS a real no-build path: prebuilt CDN/ESM web components importable from a plain <script type=module> with no bundler and no manual WASM bootstrap. Despite that, it fits none of the core requirements. It is a data-grid/pivot/charting engine for LARGE/STREAMING tabular data — a problem vc-brain explicitly does not have (small hand-curated corpus of persons/forums/evidence/briefs; 'we do NOT need market data'). It provides zero of: command-bar entity/function grammar, universal fuzzy search, tiled/linked pane manager, entity pages/routing. It has treemap/sunburst/scatter but NO node-link network graph, which is a hard requirement — a real gap. 'adapt' is impossible: the frontend logic is Rust/C++ compiled to a multi-MB WASM black box, not portable JS you can translate. The only viable option is 'adopt the whole component' as one optional pivot-grid pane over evidence tables, which ships a multi-megabyte WASM engine to render a few dozen rows and directly contradicts the zero-dependency vanilla-JS ethos. Its pivot-grid interaction design is a generic, well-known pattern that informs neither the command grammar nor the graph viz that are the actual hard parts. The candidate's own note concedes it is 'not a command bar or terminal shell' and 'heavier than the zero-dep ethos'.</reason>
<parameter name="license_confirmed">Apache-2.0
key_files_or_ideas: No-build usage (verified from docs): <script type=module> importing https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/@perspective-dev/viewer/dist/cdn/perspective-viewer.js plus viewer-datagrid, viewer-charts, and client packages; CDN builds require no WASM bootstrapping but do NOT work with bundlers. Reusable artifact is the framework-agnostic <perspective-viewer> Custom Element (grid + D3FC/Hypergrid charts) backed by a C++/Rust->WASM streaming query engine. Notable GAP for our purpose: no node-link network-graph plugin. Only plausible vc-brain use is a single heavyweight optional pivot-grid pane over evidence/signal tables — peripheral to the command-bar terminal shell and contrary to the zero-dep vanilla ethos. Verified against github.com/finos/perspective (v4.5.2, 2026-07-09) and perspective-dev.github.io guide.

name: Sigma.js (+ Graphology)
reason: VERIFIED. License MIT for both sigma.js and graphology (LICENSE.txt: "Copyright (C) 2013-2025, Alexis Jacomy, Guillaume Plique, Benoit Simard") — safe to vendor. Actively maintained, not just marketing: sigma v3.0.3 published 2026-04-30 (v4 alpha in progress), graphology 0.26.0 released 2025-02-08. Zero-dep/no-build compatible: official quickstart documents plain <script> CDN usage of sigma.min.js + graphology.umd.min.js with no bundler; the UMD bundles can be vendored as static files, matching vc-brain's vanilla-JS SPA constraint (React binding is optional). Consumed as a library, not ported — hence adopt, usable nearly as-is for the network-graph pane. Scope caveat: it is a WebGL viz primitive only (persons/forums/evidence as nodes at scale), NOT a terminal UI — it does nothing for the command bar, fuzzy search, or tiled/linked panes; pair it with other components for those. Second caveat: graphology's standalone browser bundle "often lags behind and is not always completely up to date" and exposes globals rather than clean UMD, so pin/vendor a known-good build if you need the newest std-library algorithms (community detection, pathfinding, force layouts) client-side.
key_files_or_ideas: Vendor two UMD static files: sigma.min.js (WebGL renderer) + graphology.umd.min.js (graph data model). Instantiate: build a graphology Graph (addNode/addEdge with x/y/size/color/label attrs), then `new Sigma(graph, containerEl)`. For layouts/algorithms pull from graphology's standard library (graphology-layout-forceatlas2, graphology-communities-louvain, graphology-shortest-path) — vendor a pinned standalone build since latest browser bundle lags. Good fit for the network-graph pane: persons/forums/evidence as nodes, thousands of nodes via WebGL. Does NOT provide command-bar/fuzzy-search/pane-tiling; source those elsewhere.

name: Cytoscape.js
reason: Verified against the actual repo/registry: MIT license (confirmed LICENSE + docs), actively maintained (release v3.34.0 on 2026-06-02, latest commit 2026-06-29), zero runtime dependencies, pure vanilla JS. Critically, no-build consumer usage is real: a pre-built single-file UMD dist (dist/cytoscape.min.js / cytoscape.umd.js) loads via a plain script tag with no build system — an early repo read saying 'build step required' referred to building from source as a contributor, not to consuming the library. This fits vc-brain's zero-dependency, no-npm vanilla SPA cleanly and is directly adoptable for the entity/relationship network-graph pane. Caveats: it is a viz primitive only (nothing for command-bar grammar, fuzzy search, or tiled panes); dist is ~342KB min / ~106KB gzip (single self-contained file, but not as 'lightweight' as marketed); canvas rendering degrades near ~10k nodes, which is well within the corpus scale here.
key_files_or_ideas: Drop-in single-file build: dist/cytoscape.min.js (UMD) via script tag exposes global `cytoscape()` factory; ESM build dist/cytoscape.esm.min.js for module usage. Use ONLY for the entity/relationship network-graph pane — instantiate on a container div with `elements` (nodes/edges), a `style` array (Bloomberg-terminal look achievable via node/edge CSS-like selectors), and a layout (cose/breadthfirst/concentric/dagre-via-extension). Built-in graph algorithms (shortest path, BFS/DFS, degree/centrality) support relationship queries between persons/forums. Use cy.batch() for bulk updates; hideEdgesOnViewport/textureOnViewport for large-graph perf. NOT a solution for command bar, universal search, or tiled/linked panes — pair with a separate lightweight approach for those.

name: OpenBB (Platform + Workspace)
reason: Verified: the open repo is 100% Python (languages endpoint = 8,884,110 bytes Python, zero JS/TS/HTML/CSS), and the Workspace/Pro UI is proprietary/closed and hosted at pro.openbb.co. There is NO liftable frontend code, so 'adopt'/'adapt' are impossible for a frontend judgement. License confirmed AGPLv3 directly from the LICENSE file (GitHub API shows NOASSERTION only due to a header note); AGPL copyleft would encumber copied server code but is irrelevant to a from-scratch vanilla-JS frontend that only mimics a pattern. Activity confirmed very active (pushed 2026-07-18, 70.7k stars, release Apr 2026). The value for vc-brain is purely the design spec: the widgets.json widget-contract (name/description/category/type/endpoint, gridData w/h/minW for tiled panes, column field/headerName/cellDataType/chartDataType specs) is a well-documented model for vc-brain's pane/function registry and tiled/linked-pane grammar — worth studying and reimplementing zero-dep, not copying. name: Dockview (dockview-core)
reason: All load-bearing claims verified against the real published artifacts, not marketing. GitHub confirms MIT license, v7.0.2 (Jun 22 2026), 119 releases, 3.3k stars, actively maintained. jsDelivr's data API for dockview-core@7.0.2 confirms the exact files claimed: dist/dockview-core.min.js (466KB, embedded CSS), dist/dockview-core.min.noStyle.js (339KB), dist/styles/dockview.css (124KB). Critical verification: the bundle head is a genuine UMD wrapper that, absent CommonJS/AMD, assigns to globalThis["dockview-core"] — so it loads from a plain <script> tag with NO build step and exposes a real browser global. Zero runtime dependencies confirmed (no external requires in the factory), matching our zero-dependency constraint even though the bundle itself is large. It supplies exactly the tiled/linked panes, split/grid layouts, drag-and-drop tab groups, floating + popout windows, and api.toJSON()/fromJSON() workspace persistence (localStorage/DB) for the Bloomberg-style read surface — initialized directly on a plain div. Caveats: global is hyphenated (bracket access only), it is heavy (~466KB JS + 124KB CSS) for a zero-dep vanilla project, and it covers ONLY the pane/docking layer — command bar, fuzzy search, entity pages, and network graph remain ours to build. For the docking/pane requirement specifically it is a clean, license-clean, no-build drop-in, so: adopt.
key_files_or_ideas: Consume the prebuilt UMD bundle directly, no npm/build: script-tag dist/dockview-core.min.js (466KB, CSS embedded) OR dist/dockview-core.min.noStyle.js (339KB) + dist/styles/dockview.css (124KB) from cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/dockview-core@7.0.2/ (vendor locally). Access global via window["dockview-core"] (hyphenated — bracket notation). Core API: createDockview(element, options) mounts on a plain div; api.addPanel(), tab groups, splitview/gridview/paneview, floating panels, popout windows; api.toJSON()/fromJSON() for save/restore workspaces to localStorage or DB. Provides the tiled/linked-pane + detachable-window layer only; build command bar, fuzzy search, entity pages, and network graph separately.

name: Golden Layout
reason: Verified against the live repo, npm registry, and the 2.6.0 tarball. MIT (confirmed via GitHub API spdx_id, npm, LICENSE) — no license barrier. It is the canonical Bloomberg/IDE multi-window docking pattern (row/column/stack/component tree, drag-tab-between-panels, splits, maximize, native popout, JSON layout save/load), directly on-point for our "tiled/linked panes" need. BUT it fails the zero-build constraint decisively: the npm package ships CJS + a 35-file ESM dist with EXTENSIONLESS relative imports (e.g. from './config/resolved-config'), which native browser <script type=module> cannot resolve. There is NO UMD/global build, and no unpkg/browser/exports fields. The single-file bundle (dist/bundle/esm/golden-layout.min.js) is explicitly NOT built by default and NOT included in npm — it requires `npm run build:bundles` (webpack). Maintainers say to "consume through NPM and webpack"; issue #845 documents users failing the no-build path. So it cannot drop into our vanilla-JS no-build SPA without a bundler or an external ESM CDN (network dep, against the vendored zero-dep philosophy). Porting the full layout engine to vanilla JS is impractical. Net: study its docking interaction model + JSON layout schema as the reference design; the MIT-licensed plain-CSS themes are a bonus liftable asset. Activity note: marketing 'dormant' is overstated — no stable release since v2.6.0 (2022-09-26), but master/dev last committed 2026-01-25 (docs), repo not archived, maintenance-only.
key_files_or_ideas: Design/spec to study: the docking layout tree (row/column/stack/component), drag-tab-between-panels, splitters, maximize, popout windows, and especially the JSON layout serialization schema (save/load of pane arrangement) — reimplement a simpler vanilla version informed by this. Directly liftable without a build: the MIT plain-CSS themes at dist/css/ (goldenlayout-base.css + themes/goldenlayout-dark|light|soda|translucent|borderless-dark-theme.css). Do NOT attempt to consume the JS library: npm 2.6.0 ships CJS + 35-file ESM with extensionless imports (won't load in native browser ESM), no UMD/global, no unpkg/exports; the single-file bundle must be built via `npm run build:bundles` (webpack). GitHub: golden-layout/golden-layout, docs at golden-layout.github.io/golden-layout.

name: Lumino (formerly PhosphorJS)
reason: Verified against the live repo (last commit 2026-07-16, active, 761 stars, not archived). License is genuinely BSD-3-Clause (GitHub's NOASSERTION is only due to the "Project Jupyter Contributors" copyright line; LICENSE text is verbatim BSD-3-Clause) — permissive and compatible. The candidate's key technical claim is WRONG in our favor and against it: UMD script-tag bundles DO exist (every package's unpkg/jsdelivr = dist/index.min.js, verified UMD wrapper exposing globals like lumino_widgets), so no bundler is strictly required. BUT the per-package UMD bundles do NOT inline transitive deps — @lumino/widgets alone expects 11 sibling globals (lumino_algorithm, lumino_coreutils, lumino_dragdrop, lumino_commands, etc.) preloaded, so a no-build path means vendoring/ordering ~12 script tags AND coding against Lumino's Widget/MessageLoop/Signaling/DockLayout framework plus its @lumino/default-theme CSS, which conflicts with our bespoke skeleton+skin CSS. DockPanel is deeply coupled to the Widget lifecycle and cannot be extracted as a snippet; adopting it is all-or-nothing framework buy-in that abandons the project's zero-dependency, vanilla-DOM, hand-rolled-CSS character. Its true value is as a top-tier DESIGN reference: DockPanel's drag-to-split / tabbed-group / drop-zone docking interaction is exactly the tiled/linked-pane model a Bloomberg-style terminal needs, and CommandRegistry (command IDs + keybindings + CommandPalette) is a strong pattern for the entity-and-function command bar. Study and re-implement in vanilla; do not import the framework.
license_confirmed: BSD-3-Clause (verified from LICENSE file text; GitHub shows NOASSERTION only due to custom copyright line)
key_files_or_ideas: Design references worth studying (not importing): packages/widgets/src/docklayout.ts and dockpanel.ts (drag-to-split, tabbed groups, drop-zone geometry, layout serialization) for tiled/linked panes; packages/widgets/src/tabbar.ts + packages/dragdrop for tab-drag reordering; packages/commands (CommandRegistry: command IDs, keybinding parsing/matching, isEnabled/isVisible) + packages/widgets/src/commandpalette.ts for the entity-and-function command bar; packages/datagrid (canvas-virtualized grid) as a pattern reference for dense terminal tables. Distribution note verified: UMD at https://unpkg.com/@lumino/widgets@2.9.0/dist/index.min.js exposes globalThis.lumino_widgets but requires the 11 dep globals preloaded first.

name: dock-spawn-ts
reason:
VERIFIED against live sources. License: MIT, "Copyright (c) 2019 node-projects" (LICENSE file confirmed) — permissive, attribution-only, fully adoptable. Activity: NOT abandoned — pushed_at 2026-05-19 (~2 months before eval), created 2019, 397 commits, 146 stars, 34 forks, 18 open issues, not archived. Repo size ~4.7 MB (includes images + sourcemaps).

CANDIDATE CLAIM CORRECTIONS: (1) The claim "no formal npm release tags / no versioned releases" is FALSE — dock-spawn-ts is actively published to npm with 56 versions, latest 3.22.0. This is a correction IN THE PROJECT'S FAVOR (more mature than described). (2) The claim it "ships a compiled ES5 build (lib/es5)" is TRUE for the npm distribution but MISLEADING about the git repo: the GitHub tree has NO lib/es5 folder (lib/ holds css/, images/, js/); the es5 global bundle is a build artifact generated at publish time via esbuild, present only in the npm tarball. The es5 demo (page/demo/demo_simple_es5.html) references ../../lib/es5/dock-spawn-ts.js, which resolves only after install/build.

NO-BUILD COMPATIBLE — TWO CONFIRMED PATHS: (a) plain <script src="lib/es5/dock-spawn-ts.js"> exposing global DockSpawnTS (vendor the single npm bundle; no npm/build needed to consume); (b) checked-in ES modules under lib/js/*.js used via <script type="module"> with relative .js imports — verified DockManager.js imports "./Dialog.js" etc., browser-resolvable with NO bundler. Runtime dependencies: ZERO (package.json dependencies: none; build tools are devDeps only). So it drops into the zero-dep vanilla SPA cleanly.

FIT / SCOPE CAVEATS (important): dock-spawn-ts is a VS-Code-style IDE DOCKING framework (drag tabs, undock into floating dialogs, nested resizable splits). It addresses ONLY the "tiled/linked panes" requirement — it provides nothing for the command bar, fuzzy search, entity pages, or network graph. Its interaction model is heavier than a typical Bloomberg fixed linked-tile grid, and it ships its own opinionated CSS (dock-manager-style.css) + image assets (dock indicators) that must be reconciled/re-skinned to fit the existing skeleton+skin system. Verdict "adopt" applies to the pane-layout LAYER only, not the whole read surface.
key_files_or_ideas: lib/es5/dock-spawn-ts.js (npm-only global bundle, <script> drop-in exposing DockSpawnTS); lib/js/*.js (checked-in ES modules, type=module import, no build); page/demo/demo_simple.html (ESM no-build example) and page/demo/demo_simple_es5.html (global-script example); core API DockManager + PanelContainer; DockGraphSerializer/DockGraphDeserializer for saving/restoring pane layouts (useful for persisting terminal workspaces); lib/css/dock-manager*.css + lib/images = the skin/assets to override for skeleton+skin integration.

name: WinBox.js
reason: Verified against the real repo, not just marketing. LICENSE file confirms Apache-2.0 (permissive, safe to vendor into a proprietary static SPA). Zero-dependency vanilla JS, no build step required: dist/winbox.bundle.min.js exists and is genuinely tiny (15,984 bytes raw, ~5.7KB gzip as claimed), usable via a single <script> tag. This makes it a clean drop-in that fits our zero-dep/no-build constraint perfectly. VERDICT IS SCOPED, NOT BLANKET: WinBox is a free-floating, overlapping window manager (draggable/resizable/min/max/fullscreen/modal + popout iframes). It is adopt-able as-is ONLY for the detachable/popout-pane slice of the terminal. It does NOT provide the tiling/docking-tree layout that a Bloomberg terminal's 'tiled/linked panes' really wants, and it contributes nothing to the command bar, fuzzy search, entity pages, or network graph -- those must come from elsewhere. Maintenance caveat: personal side project, last commit Dec 21 2023 (~2.5yr stale as of 2026), so treat it as a frozen vendored file (Apache-2.0 lets us fork if needed) rather than a living dependency. Net: adopt verbatim if/when we want floating popout windows; it is not a foundation for the core layout.
key_files_or_ideas: dist/winbox.bundle.min.js (single-file, script-tag drop-in, ~16KB raw); or split dist/js/winbox.min.js (10.9KB) + dist/css/winbox.min.css (4.8KB). Reusable interaction ideas: window drag/resize handles, z-index focus stacking, min/max/fullscreen controls, mount DOM/HTML/iframe into a pane, splitscreen snapping, custom event callbacks (onclose/onfocus/onresize) -- useful as a pattern reference for detachable panes even if we build our own tiling layer.

name: Gridstack.js
reason: Verified adoptable as the TILED-PANE layout layer, though it covers only one sub-requirement. License MIT confirmed via GitHub API spdx_id AND package.json license field. Zero runtime deps CONFIRMED: package.json dependencies:{} — all 31 deps are devDependencies (webpack/grunt/vitest/eslint tooling only). No-build usage CONFIRMED: ships dist/gridstack-all.js single-file bundle (84,793 bytes) + dist/gridstack.min.css (3,845 bytes), usable via plain <script>+<link> tags with GridStack.init(); no npm/bundler needed. Drops cleanly into our zero-dep vanilla static SPA. Very active: pushed today (2026-07-19), v13.0.0 being cut now, last tag v12.6.0 (Apr 2026), not archived, maintained by adumesny; ~9k stars all match claims (claim of v12.6.0 is slightly stale/conservative, not inflated). SCOPE CAVEAT (adversarial): Gridstack solves ONLY the drag/resize grid-of-cards with JSON serialize-restore — good for tiled linked panes and saving workspace layouts — but provides NONE of: command-bar entity+function grammar, fuzzy search, entity pages, or network-graph viz. It is a dashboard-GRID paradigm, not IDE-style docking (no tab groups / split-dock tree). Used as-is (include the one file), not a code-port. Complements, does not replace, the command-bar/search/graph work.
key_files_or_ideas: dist/gridstack-all.js (single-file zero-dep bundle, ~85KB) + dist/gridstack.min.css (~3.8KB) — include via script/link tags, no build. API: GridStack.init(opts) then grid.addWidget({w,h,x,y,content}). Layout persistence: grid.save()/load() serializes pane layout to JSON — directly reusable for saving/restoring terminal workspace arrangements. Paradigm: responsive x/y grid of rearrangeable cards (tiled, not docked); nested grids supported. Use ONLY for the pane-tiling layer; pair with a separate command-bar, fuzzy-search, and graph solution.

name: Split.js
reason: Verified drop-in for the resizable-split-pane slice of the tiled/linked-pane layout. LICENSE: MIT confirmed three ways (GitHub API spdx_id=MIT, package README, root README) — clean to adopt verbatim. NO-BUILD: I fetched and read the actual distributed file (unpkg split.js@1.6.0/dist/split.min.js). It is a single self-contained UMD (~4.8KB minified / ~1-2KB gzip), pure browser DOM APIs, ZERO runtime dependencies, exposes window.Split. You vendor that one file into the static SPA and load it with a plain <script> tag — no npm, no bundler, no CDN needed (important since it must live locally under the artifact CSP). API is trivial: Split(['#a','#b'], {sizes, minSize, gutterSize, direction:'horizontal'|'vertical', onDrag}). SKEPTICAL FINDINGS: (1) The monorepo itself DOES have a build step (Lerna + Svelte docs site, JS 51.9% / Svelte 42.4%) — but that is only for maintainers; consumers never build it, so it does not violate the no-build constraint. (2) Activity is overstated as merely 'mature/stable' — real last push is 2024-05-06, ~2 years stale as of July 2026, with 172 open issues; not archived, but effectively frozen. For a 4.8KB dependency-free utility this staleness is low-risk (no security surface, no external deps to rot). (3) Scope is narrow and correctly described by the candidate: it is a resize-gutter primitive between sibling elements, NOT a docking/tiling manager — it gives you draggable resizable splits, but the tiled layout, pane linking, command-bar grammar, entity pages, and network graph are all still hand-rolled. Verdict is 'adopt' (not 'adapt') because you use the file as-is with no code porting; it just solves one small piece.
license_confirmed: MIT (verified via GitHub API spdx_id=MIT, plus package and root README license fields)
key_files_or_ideas: Adopt the single prebuilt UMD file packages/splitjs/dist/split.min.js (equivalently unpkg.com/split.js@1.6.0/dist/split.min.js) — ~4.8KB min / ~1-2KB gzip, self-contained, no runtime deps, defines window.Split. Vendor it locally into the SPA and load via <script>. Core API for the linked-pane resize behavior: Split([elemA, elemB, ...], {sizes:[25,75], minSize:200, gutterSize:8, direction:'horizontal', onDrag, onDragEnd}). It inserts draggable gutters between sibling flex/grid children and sizes via pure CSS. Use it only for the resizable-gutter primitive; build the tiling/docking, command bar, entity pages, and graph yourself. Split Grid (sibling package) is an alternative for CSS-grid-based layouts if you prefer grid over flex.

name: FDWjs (Floating & Docking Windows)
reason: Adversarial verification kills this candidate on multiple independent grounds. (1) LICENSE is GPL-2.0 (confirmed: "GNU GENERAL PUBLIC LICENSE Version 2, June 1991") — strong copyleft that would force vc-brain's frontend to be GPL if any code were adopted or ported; disqualifies adopt/adapt outright. (2) It is NOT vanilla/zero-dependency as the metadata claims: core fdw.js depends heavily on jQuery ($ IIFE param, $("<div/>"), .addClass/.attr/.appendTo/.css/.outerWidth/.position throughout). README author wants to drop jQuery but never did. This violates the project's zero-dependency constraint directly. (3) Abandoned: last commit 2016-03-18 (README-only); real code stopped ~2013-2015; 1 star, 1 fork. (4) Self-admitted "dirty/buggy," incomplete (right-dock only; minimize, save/load, tabs all "Soon" and never shipped), no docs, no demo, ~320-340 line core. (5) Covers only a partial docking-pane feature and none of the actual needs (command bar/entity-function grammar, fuzzy search, linked panes, entity pages, network graph). Its one relevant idea is done cleaner and zero-dependency by Dockview and dock-spawn-ts.
license_confirmed: GPL-2.0 — confirmed via repo LICENSE file header "GNU GENERAL PUBLIC LICENSE Version 2, June 1991". Copyleft; incompatible with adopting/porting into the project.
key_files_or_ideas: Files: fdw.js (core WindowManager, jQuery-bound), dockwindow.js, DockContainer.js, Movable.js, Splitter.js, fdw.css, index.html demo. Only conceptual idea of value is the floating-window + right-dock splitter pane model, but it is jQuery-dependent, GPL-licensed, and better implemented by Dockview/dock-spawn-ts — nothing here worth porting.

name: FlexLayout (flexlayout-react)
reason: Verified on the live repo: MIT license, actively maintained (v0.10.0 released 2026-07-14, latest commit same day), and package.json confirms an empty `dependencies` object with `react`+`react-dom` as `^18||^19` peerDependencies. It ships ONLY as a built ESM bundle (main/module = ./dist/index.js, types dir), built via Vite+SASS with pnpm/npm scripts — there is no UMD/standalone/no-build artifact. So it is a hard React + build-step dependency and cannot be dropped into or lifted from our zero-dependency vanilla no-build SPA. Not adoptable; too React-coupled (component tree, hooks, TSX) for a clean code port. Its real value is as a top-tier interaction-design reference from an actual trading-software vendor (Caplin): the JSON-tree layout model and its docking/tab-overflow/maximize/popout patterns are directly worth studying and reimplementing in vanilla for the tiled/linked-pane terminal. MIT permits studying and reimplementing freely.
key_files_or_ideas: Design/spec worth studying (all React/TSX, not directly reusable): the framework-agnostic-ish model layer in src/model/ (Model.ts, RowNode.ts, TabSetNode.ts, TabNode.ts, BorderNode.ts, Actions.ts) which encodes the whole workspace as a serializable JSON tree and runs a doLayout pass — translatable in concept to vanilla. Interaction patterns to copy: edge/border docking via drag, splitters/resizable tabsets, tab overflow menu, maximize/restore, and popout tabs into floating panels or new browser windows. These map directly to the terminal's tiled/linked panes. Ignore its build tooling (Vite/pnpm) and React rendering — reimplement in vanilla JS.

name: rc-dock
reason: Independently verified adverse checks: React-bound with a dependency tree, alpha-only with no stable release/tags, and a lesser reference than FlexLayout/react-mosaic for the same dock/tile design space — so design-study value only.
key_files_or_ideas: Design ideas worth studying (not code to port): (1) float mode — panels that detach from the dock grid and float freely; (2) popout — moving a panel into a separate native browser window (via rc-new-window) while keeping it linked, relevant to multi-monitor terminal setups; (3) dock/split model — horizontal/vertical splits with tabbed dockable panels and drag-to-dock drop zones; (4) controlled vs uncontrolled layout state (serializable layout JSON) as a pattern for persisting/restoring our tiled-pane arrangement; (5) built-in dark theme via Less light/dark stylesheets. Repo: github.com/ticlo/rc-dock (master branch). Note: all logic is React + @rc-component/* + lodash bound; nothing is usable without a build step.

name: react-grid-layout
reason: Verified against the live repo. License is genuinely MIT (LICENSE: "Copyright (c) 2016 Samuel Reed"; package.json "license":"MIT") — permissive and compatible. Very active: v2.2.3 released 2026-03-24, 22.4k stars, TS 57.5%/JS 41.7%. The claimed framework-agnostic core is REAL: src/core/collision.ts imports only `type` from ./types (no React, ~59 lines, exports collides/getFirstCollision/getAllCollisions); sibling files compactors/layout/position/constraints/responsive are likewise pure. BUT the "directly reusable" framing overstates it for our strict no-npm/no-build vanilla SPA: the ./core export points to COMPILED dist/core.mjs (ESM) / dist/core.js (CJS), and v2 removed the UMD bundle — there is NO browser-ready standalone. So it is not drop-in; you must vendor the compiled ESM, load it from a CDN via import maps (an unwanted runtime dep), or port the small pure .ts files to plain JS. The React components (with clsx, fast-equals, prop-types, react-draggable, react-resizable peer deps) are out of scope. Also note paradigm mismatch: this is the free-form draggable widget-grid (Grafana/Metabase style), not the nested/linked-pane docking of a Bloomberg terminal. Net: not adopt (fails no-build test), but the small MIT pure algorithms and dashboard UX are worth porting/studying — adapt.
key_files_or_ideas: Port targets (pure, MIT, no framework): src/core/collision.ts (collides, getFirstCollision, getAllCollisions), src/core/compactors.ts (verticalCompactor/horizontalCompactor/noCompactor), src/core/layout.ts (moveElement + compaction), src/core/position.ts (grid<->pixel math), src/core/constraints.ts. Distribution reality: package.json exports "./core" -> dist/core.mjs (ESM only, no UMD). Reusable IDEAS for our tiled/linked panes: collision detection + vertical/horizontal compaction for auto-packing panes on an x/y grid; responsive breakpoint layout swapping; the widget-dashboard interaction model as a UX reference (draggable/resizable tiles). Caveat: it's a free-form widget grid, not nested docking, so for Bloomberg-style linked panes it's a partial reference, not a blueprint.
license_confirmed: MIT — confirmed via /LICENSE ("Copyright (c) 2016 Samuel Reed") and package.json "license":"MIT". Permissive, allows porting with attribution.

name: FINOS regular-table
reason:
Every material claim verified against the live repo, not marketing. LICENSE returns 200 and GitHub API reports SPDX Apache-2.0 (permissive, compatible with a private static SPA). Actively maintained and NOT stale: v0.8.5 tagged 2026-07-09 (10 days before today), FINOS Graduated, not archived, 18 open issues, 399 stars. Zero-dependency is real: package.json "dependencies" is an empty object; esbuild/typescript/playwright are devDependencies only. No-build usage is real and drop-in: the published dist/esm/regular-table.js (23.7 KB minified, verified minified single-line source) self-registers the element via customElements.define("regular-table", ...) as a pure import side-effect, so `<script type="module" src=".../regular-table">` + a 2.6 KB `<link>` stylesheet is the entire integration — no npm, no bundler. It renders a real DOM `<table>` styled with ordinary descendant selectors (regular-table tbody td, thead th, .rt-cell-clip, .rt-column-resize), which slots directly into the existing skeleton+skin CSS system. The async model (setDataListener returning {num_rows,num_columns,data} over an x0,y0,x1,y1 window) matches loading corpus data on demand, and you supply cell rendering + click handlers — ideal for entity tables and tiled data panes. Honest scope caveat: this is ONLY a virtual-scroll grid engine. It solves the hardest DOM-perf piece as a genuine drop-in but does nothing for the command bar, fuzzy search, entity-page layout, or network graph — those remain yours to build. Adopt for the grid/table-pane role specifically.</reason>
<parameter name="frontend_reusable">true
key_files_or_ideas:
CDN integration (no build): <script type="module" src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/regular-table@0.8.5"></script> + <link rel="stylesheet" href=".../dist/css/material.css"/>. Core API: el.setDataListener((x0,y0,x1,y1) => ({num_rows, num_columns, data})) then append <regular-table> to DOM. Self-registers via customElements.define. Style hooks: `regular-table tbody td`, `regular-table thead th`, `.rt-cell-clip`, `.rt-column-resize` (2.6 KB material.css is a swappable skin template). Bundle: dist/esm/regular-table.js = 23.7 KB minified, 0 runtime deps. No built-in sort/filter/edit — build interaction on top (they point to Perspective for those, which you don't need). Best used as the entity-table / tiled-data-pane engine, not for command bar / search / graph.</parameter>
</invoke>

name: AG Grid Community
reason: VERIFIED, with narrow scope. License MIT for Community (confirmed in-repo LICENSE, free for production; Enterprise is separate commercial). Very active (v36.0.1 released 2026-07-15, ~46.5k commits, commercially backed). No-build usage is real: single jsDelivr UMD script tag exposes global `agGrid` and `agGrid.createGrid(container, gridOptions)`, all Community modules auto-registered, CSS bundled, vendorable as one file with no npm/bundler. Adoptable as-is for exactly ONE thing: the terminal "data blotter" pane (multi-column sort, quick-filter, keyboard nav, column pin/resize, inline edit — all Community, no code to write). Caveats that keep this from being an unqualified adopt: (1) it does nothing for the distinctive terminal surface we must build — command-bar entity+function grammar, universal fuzzy search, tiled/linked panes, entity pages, network graph; (2) real size is heaviest of the grid options (~900KB full UMD Community, hundreds of KB minified — the candidate's "larger footprint" note is understated); (3) v33+ defaults to a JS Theming API that fights a hand-rolled skeleton+skin CSS system — integrating requires theme="legacy" plus manual CSS; (4) the most Bloomberg-like blotter features (row grouping, pivot, aggregation) are Enterprise/paid. It is a drop-in library, not portable snippets ("adapt" wrong) and more than a design spec ("reference" wrong). Adopt only if the team accepts one large third-party dependency for the blotter pane; otherwise skip to preserve zero-dependency purity.
license_confirmed: MIT (ag-grid-community, verified free for production use); ag-grid-enterprise is a separate commercial license
key_files_or_ideas: UMD/CDN drop-in: https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/ag-grid-community@36.0.1/dist/ag-grid-community.min.js (global `agGrid`, `agGrid.createGrid(container, gridOptions)`). Scope to the data-blotter pane only. To coexist with the skin CSS system, opt out of the default Theming API via theme="legacy" and style manually. Avoid features gated to Enterprise (row grouping/pivot/aggregation). Note it does not address command grammar, fuzzy search, linked panes, entity pages, or the network graph.

name: Tabulator
reason:
Verified against the live repo: LICENSE is MIT, "Copyright (c) 2015-2026 Oli Folkerd", no dual-licensing and no paid/Enterprise tier (the AG-Grid contrast is accurate). Latest release 6.5.2 on 2026-06-23 — genuinely very active. Consumers need NO build step: prebuilt dist/css/ and dist/js/ ship on unpkg; the repo's babel/jest/playwright tooling is contributor-only. It's a single self-contained vanilla-JS file with zero runtime deps (no jQuery), so it can be vendored locally and stays inside our no-build/no-npm constraint. Fit is partial but real: Tabulator covers only the rich-grid slice of a terminal surface — tree/grouped data maps cleanly to forum→attendant→evidence hierarchies, column formatters render entity chips and click-to-link cells for the linked-pane model, and it's CSS-skinnable to our skeleton+skin system, with sorting/filtering/export/virtual-rendering free. It does NOT provide the command bar, entity-function grammar, universal fuzzy search, tiled-pane manager, entity pages, or network graph — those stay build-it-yourself. Adopt it as the grid component, not as a blueprint for the terminal shell. Minor caveat: the "~80–120 KB" claim is gzipped size; raw min.js is ~400 KB. Only friction is that a zero-dependency project takes on one external library, but it's a clean single MIT file.</parameter>
<parameter name="key_files_or_ideas">Adopt prebuilt dist/js/tabulator.min.js + a themeable dist/css variant (vendored locally, ~400KB raw / ~120KB gzip). Key reusable capabilities: tree/grouped data (Data Tree, group rows) for forum→attendant→evidence hierarchies; custom column formatters to emit entity-chip HTML and click-to-link cells feeding the linked-pane model; built-in sort/filter/pagination/export; progressive virtual rendering for large evidence sets; full CSS-variable theming to match the skeleton+skin system. Does NOT cover: command bar, fuzzy search, pane tiling/linking, entity pages, graph viz — build those separately.</parameter>
<parameter name="license_confirmed">MIT (verified in LICENSE file: "The MIT License (MIT), Copyright (c) 2015-2026 Oli Folkerd"). No dual-licensing, no paid tier, no Enterprise gating.

name: Grid.js
reason:
Verified real and legit but a poor architectural fit. License MIT (confirmed on repo). No-build CDN usage confirmed: `<script src="unpkg.com/gridjs/dist/gridjs.umd.js">` + theme `<link>` exposes a global `Grid` usable in vanilla JS with no npm/build. Measured size (not marketing): gridjs.umd.js = 53KB raw / 16.6KB gzip + mermaid.min.css 7.8KB (the "~25KB" claim is roughly right on gzip). BUT: (1) last real release is v6.2.0, March 2024 — ~2.3yrs stale, near-dormant cadence (2026 CDN last-modified is just a re-cache, not a code change). (2) It renders via a BUNDLED PREACT virtual DOM, so it is itself a third-party runtime dependency — adopting it breaks the project's defining zero-dependency, hand-rolled-vanilla-DOM property. (3) Its "mermaid" theme CSS conflicts with the skeleton+skin system and needs heavy override to read as a terminal skin. (4) The candidate's marquee "universal search" relevance is OVERSTATED: Grid.js search is per-table filtering, not the cross-entity command-bar/fuzzy search the terminal needs. For a Bloomberg-style surface where tables are secondary panes, a hand-rolled sortable <table> fits the zero-dep architecture better than an opinionated Preact widget.</reason>
<parameter name="license_confirmed">MIT
key_files_or_ideas: Zero-build CDN drop-in: <script src="https://unpkg.com/gridjs/dist/gridjs.umd.js"></script> + <link href="https://unpkg.com/gridjs/dist/theme/mermaid.min.css" rel="stylesheet"> exposes global `Grid`. Note it bundles Preact (verified via umd source map), so it is a runtime dependency, not vanilla DOM. Sort/search/paginate UX is standard, nothing novel to port.

name: canvas-datagrid
reason: Verified BSD-3-Clause (LICENSE: "Copyright (c) 2016, Tony Germaneri", all 3 clauses) and a genuine no-build web component (dist/canvas-datagrid.js fetched HTTP 200; self-contained webpack UMD, 248KB min / 63KB gzip, exposes global canvasDatagrid + <canvas-datagrid> element). So it literally meets the license + no-build bar. But it is a poor fit for our purpose and I recommend against it: (1) SKIN CONFLICT — canvas-rendered text cannot inherit CSS variables; theming is only via a JS style object, which directly fights the project's core skeleton+skin CSS architecture (the candidate flags this and it is confirmed). (2) SCALE IRRELEVANT — its only differentiator is millions of rows on one canvas to avoid DOM-node limits; our corpus (persons/forums/evidence/briefs) is hundreds-to-low-thousands of rows, where a plain skinnable, accessible HTML table serves the blotter better. (3) NARROW — it is only a grid pane; contributes nothing to the command-bar grammar, universal fuzzy search, tiled/linked panes, entity pages, or network-graph. (4) NOT PORTABLE IN PIECES — monolithic 248KB canvas engine, all-or-nothing, no snippets to translate out. Activity is low: npm v0.4.7 is stale (May 2023) though master had two commits in Sept 2025 (last: Sept 18 2025); 142 open issues.
key_files_or_ideas: dist/canvas-datagrid.js (self-contained UMD bundle, 248KB min / 63KB gzip, loads via <script>, global canvasDatagrid + <canvas-datagrid> web component). Theming is done through a JS style object (grid.style.*), NOT CSS — cannot inherit skeleton+skin CSS variables. Single inlined runtime dep is-printable-key-event@1.0.0. npm tarball is 3.69MB unpacked but that is source+tutorials+tests, not the runtime artifact.

name: RevoGrid
reason:
Verified legit but poor fit. License: MIT core CONFIRMED (LICENSE file present; docs say "free forever"), with a real commercial Pro split (formulas/charts/Excel/Tree/Pivot/Gantt/AI are paid). Very active (v4.23.22, Jul 11 2026; ~1,970 commits; 3.4k stars). No-build usage CONFIRMED: ships as a standalone Stencil Web Component loadable via plain <script> from jsDelivr/unpkg or ESM defineCustomElement(), then <revo-grid> in HTML — genuinely compatible with the vanilla/no-build model. HOWEVER, adversarial fit is weak: (1) Stencil renders in SHADOW DOM, so it can only be themed through whatever CSS custom properties/parts it exposes — the project's global skeleton+skin CSS cannot fully reach inside, fighting the skin system. (2) It is a heavy spreadsheet with cell editing, range selection, and virtualization — dead weight on a READ-ONLY corpus of persons/forums/evidence/briefs that won't have millions of rows. (3) It is a compiled library you consume whole; there is no separable source to port or "adapt." (4) It addresses none of the terminal's hard parts (command-bar entity+function grammar, universal fuzzy search, tiled/linked panes, entity pages, network graph) — only a commodity tabular pane the project can render natively with plain HTML tables plus its own skin CSS. Adopting it means bolting a large opaque third-party dependency onto a deliberately zero-dependency SPA to solve the easiest sub-problem.</reason>
<parameter name="license_confirmed">MIT (core) — verified: LICENSE file present at revolist/revogrid/blob/main/LICENSE; docs confirm Community tier is MIT and "free forever." Separate commercial Pro tier for advanced features. No copyleft/attribution risk for the free core.
key_files_or_ideas: No-build entry points if ever reconsidered for a heavy virtualized data pane: CDN script `https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/@revolist/revogrid@latest/dist/revo-grid.js` or ESM `import { defineCustomElement as defineRevoGrid } from 'https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/@revolist/revogrid@latest/standalone/revo-grid.js/+esm'; defineRevoGrid();` then `<revo-grid>`. Theming limited to RevoGrid-exposed CSS custom properties / ::part (shadow DOM). Free-core features: virtual scroll, cell edit, sort, basic filter, range selection; Pro-only: formulas, charts, Excel export, Tree View, Pivot, Gantt, AI. Only worth revisiting (as an "adopt") if the corpus needs a truly virtualized 100k+ row grid pane; for a read-only skin-driven surface, native tables are the better fit.

name: Glide Data Grid
reason: Verified against the actual repo. License MIT (confirmed). Genuinely active: last commit on main Jan 21, 2026 — though last STABLE npm release is 6.0.3 (Feb 2024); main sits at 6.0.4-alpha25. Technically incompatible with our zero-dep vanilla no-build SPA: packages/core/package.json peerDependencies hard-require react + react-dom (16-19) plus lodash/marked/react-responsive-carousel; runtime deps include @linaria/react (build-time CSS-in-JS). Ships ESM/CJS only (main=dist/cjs, module/browser=dist/esm) with NO unpkg/UMD/script-tag build and a required dist/index.css import — so a bundler is mandatory and there is no vanilla path. Cannot adopt or reasonably adapt (porting means unwinding React + Linaria). Reference value is real but NARROWER than the candidate's pitch: its core strength is canvas rendering of millions of tabular rows, which is not central to a command-bar/fuzzy-search/linked-pane/network-graph terminal. Worth studying only for one slice — a dense, keyboard-navigable, copy/paste screener/list pane — hence reference, not skip.
key_files_or_ideas: Design/interaction patterns only (no code is portable): canvas-drawn cell rendering for smooth scroll under large datasets; custom cell renderer abstraction (per-column draw functions); native-feeling keyboard navigation and range selection; spreadsheet-style copy/paste of selected ranges. Relevant solely if we add a dense screener/list pane. Everything is React + @linaria/react in packages/core — treat as spec to reimplement in vanilla, never a dependency.

name: DataTables
reason:
Verified on the live repo: core is MIT (fine) and it loads via CDN with no build step, latest release v2.3.7 dated 2026-01-30 (actively maintained, ~7.4k stars). BUT the disqualifier is confirmed: jQuery is a hard, mandatory dependency. DataTables 2.0 explicitly did NOT drop jQuery — it kept jQuery as its single required dependency and only renamed the file (jquery.dataTables.js -> dataTables.js), which spawned false 'jQuery removed' rumors. Requiring jQuery directly violates vc-brain's zero-dependency vanilla-JS ethos. The Editor add-on (in-table editing) is separately commercial/paid, undercutting the free-interactions value. The only capability it provides (sortable/filterable/paginated list pane) is trivial in vanilla JS or available jQuery-free via Tabulator / Grid.js / Simple-DataTables. Its code is a jQuery-plugin architecture — the exact pattern we're avoiding — so nothing is worth porting, and its sort/filter/paginate UX is industry-standard, not distinctive enough to study as a reference.</reason>
<parameter name="license_confirmed">MIT (core, confirmed on repo LICENSE); some extensions (Editor) separately commercial/paid
key_files_or_ideas: None worth adopting. Requires jQuery (mandatory single dependency in v2.x — not removed). Loads via CDN with no build step, but only when jQuery is also loaded. For a list/table pane in a zero-dep vanilla SPA, prefer Tabulator, Grid.js, or Simple-DataTables, or hand-roll sort/filter/paginate. Repo: github.com/DataTables/DataTables; current dev in DataTables/DataTablesSrc.

name: Handsontable
reason:
Verified against the actual LICENSE.txt and repo docs: this is a proprietary DUAL license, not permissive OSS. Free only via the 'non-commercial-and-evaluation' key (personal/eval/academic); commercial use requires a paid license from handsoncode, plus an anti-competitive clause forbidding building competing software. That is a hard blocker for a commercial product — you cannot legally adopt OR port its code into a commercial build. Separately, relevance is weak: Handsontable is a spreadsheet-EDITING grid, whereas we need a command bar, fuzzy search, linked panes, entity pages, and a network graph — none of which it provides. Its only overlap (a data-grid pane) is better served by MIT-licensed Tabulator/RevoGrid. Repo is very active (v18.0.0 released 2026-06-30) but that does not change the license or fit.</reason>
<parameter name="license_confirmed">Proprietary dual license (Handsoncode). Confirmed via master/LICENSE.txt and repo docs: free non-commercial/evaluation key only; commercial use requires a purchased license; includes an anti-competitive-use clause. NOT MIT/BSD/Apache and NOT usable in a commercial product without payment.
key_files_or_ideas: Only conceptually relevant: the notion of a spreadsheet/data-grid pane. No code is reusable due to the license. If a grid pane is ever needed, evaluate MIT-licensed Tabulator or RevoGrid instead. Command bar, fuzzy search, linked panes, and network-graph requirements are entirely outside Handsontable's scope.

name: TradingView Lightweight Charts
reason: Verified clean technically but wrong domain for our purpose. It is exclusively a time-series financial-chart RENDERER (line/area/candlestick/OHLC/histogram/baseline on HTML5 canvas) and contributes nothing to the terminal's defining features: command-bar grammar, fuzzy search, tiled/linked panes, entity pages, and — critically — network-graph visualization (which it cannot do; it is time-series only). The candidate's own pitch concedes "we don't need market data" and falls back to a speculative peripheral use (evidence-signal timelines / score trajectories in a chart pane) that is not an established need for our persons/forums/evidence/briefs corpus. Adopting it also conflicts with the explicit ZERO-DEPENDENCY vanilla-JS/no-npm constraint (it is a ~40KB third-party lib), and its Apache-2.0 license carries a MANDATORY attribution requirement — a TradingView NOTICE + link to tradingview.com must be displayed on any user-facing page — an odd encumbrance for an optional chart pane. Its frontend code is optimized canvas TypeScript (used wholesale, not ported), and its interaction design (crosshair/price-time scales) is time-series-specific with little to teach our command/pane/graph surface. Skip.

NOTICE file (verified): "TradingView Lightweight Charts(TM) Copyright (c) 2025 TradingView, Inc. https://www.tradingview.com/". Only mildly transferable design idea: its support for syncing crosshair/time-scale across multiple chart instances loosely echoes our "linked panes" goal, but this is thin and time-series-specific.
