name: uPlot
reason: Verification PASSED on every claim: MIT license (confirmed), last commit 2026-04-22 (actively maintained), ~50 KB vanilla-JS + Canvas 2D with zero dependencies, and a prebuilt IIFE bundle (dist/uPlot.iife.min.js + required uPlot.min.css) that is genuinely usable via a <script> tag with no build step. It is adopt-grade technically. But it is a category mismatch for this project: uPlot is a Cartesian time-series/OHLC/line/bar plotter, and our required frontend patterns are a command-bar grammar, universal fuzzy search, tiled/linked panes, entity pages, and a network (node-link) graph — none of which uPlot provides. It cannot render network graphs at all. Its single strength, dense time-series/sparkline panes, is exactly what the task explicitly excludes ("We do NOT need market data"); the corpus is a knowledge graph, not time-series signals. The only overlap (cursor-sync/linked charts) maps weakly to "linked panes" and is trivial to reimplement without the library. Skip because it solves a problem we do not have.
key_files_or_ideas: dist/uPlot.iife.min.js + dist/uPlot.min.css (drop-in, no build); dist/uPlot.esm.js, uPlot.cjs.js, uPlot.d.ts. Design idea with marginal relevance: uPlot's cursor-sync / linked-charts pattern (multiple plots sharing a synced cursor) loosely informs a "linked panes" concept, but it is not needed for our non-time-series knowledge-graph surface.

name: Apache ECharts
reason: VERIFIED. License Apache-2.0 (confirmed on repo) — permissive, reuse-clean. Very active: v6.0.0 (2025-07-30), v6.1.0 (2026-05-19), top-level Apache project, ~66.8k stars. No-build claim CONFIRMED: dist/echarts.min.js is a self-contained UMD bundle on jsDelivr, loads via a plain <script> tag, no npm/bundler, and bundles its own renderer (zrender) so it pulls in no runtime dep tree. Network-graph CONFIRMED: series.type:'graph', layout:'force', nodes/links/categories/roam/repulsion — directly serves the entity-network requirement; treemap/heatmap/sunburst cover evidence/coverage panes. SCOPE: adopt ONLY as the graph/chart RENDERER — it contributes NO shell or interaction design (no command bar, no fuzzy search, no tiled/linked panes, no entity pages), i.e. it addresses one of the requirements, not the terminal shell. CAVEATS the candidate glossed: (1) no-build path = full ~1MB min bundle (~335KB gzip); the 'slimmer custom build' REQUIRES a bundler/build step, so no-build and slim are mutually exclusive. (2) Themes via JS theme objects, NOT CSS — will not participate in the skeleton+skin system; skin switching needs a JS theme-swap bridge. (3) Adopting a 1MB library ends the project's ZERO-DEPENDENCY property. Net: a legitimate license-clean, no-build drop-in for the network graph specifically, if the team accepts the weight and the CSS-theming mismatch.
key_files_or_ideas: dist/echarts.min.js (UMD, jsDelivr <script>, no build); series.type:'graph' with layout:'force' (nodes=data, links={source,target}, categories, roam, repulsion/gravity/edgeLength) for the entity network; treemap/heatmap/sunburst for evidence/coverage panes. Do NOT look to it for shell/command-bar/search/pane code — it has none. Slim build requires echarts/core tree-shaking + a bundler (breaks the no-build path).

name: Chart.js
reason: Verified: MIT (LICENSE.md, "The MIT License (MIT)", Chart.js Contributors 2014-2024) and no-build compatible via a self-contained UMD bundle (dist/chart.umd.min.js) loadable from a single script tag with no npm/build and no transitive runtime deps — so it drops into the zero-dep vanilla-JS static SPA as one vendored file. Activity claim "Very active" is overstated: it is maintenance-mode/stable — latest release v4.5.1 (Oct 13, 2025), most recent commit May 27, 2026, prior commits Apr 16, 2026 and Nov 24, 2025 — alive but low-frequency, not abandoned. ~60-65 KB gzipped, matching the ~60 KB claim. FIT CAVEAT: Chart.js is a black-box charting lib and contributes zero interaction design to the hard parts of this project (command-bar grammar, fuzzy search, tiled/linked panes, entity pages) and has NO network-graph type (scatter is not a node-link graph) — the graph-viz requirement must be solved by a dedicated lib. Its real value is the narrow "no-market-data" slice: simple score/distribution/category-bar/scatter panes for briefs and evidence signals. Adopt for that pane role only; defaults are animated/rounded and need restyling (disable animation, monospace ticks, terminal grid colors) for Bloomberg density, and it does add a ~65 KB dependency to an otherwise zero-dependency codebase.
key_files_or_ideas: dist/chart.umd.min.js — self-contained UMD build, no build step, loads via one script tag (cdnjs/jsDelivr) or vendored locally. API: `new Chart(canvasCtx, config)` for line/bar/pie/scatter/bubble/radar. For terminal density set options.animation=false, monospace tick fonts, dark grid/border colors. Use only for simple score/distribution/category-bar panes in briefs and evidence signals. chartjs-chart-financial plugin (candlestick/OHLC) exists but is NOT needed here (no market data). Does NOT cover: command bar, fuzzy search, pane layout/linking, entity pages, or network-graph visualization — pair with separate libs for those.

name: Plotly.js
reason:
Verified: MIT license (confirmed on repo), genuinely active (v3.7.0 released 2026-07-03, monthly cadence), and truly no-build (single UMD global via cdn.plot.ly, no npm). But it fails the fit test for our purpose. It is a monolithic charting library that provides NONE of the four core terminal features (command-bar entity/function grammar, universal fuzzy search, tiled/linked panes, entity pages). Its only arguably-relevant capability, network graphs, is weak: Plotly has no force-directed layout engine, so you must hand-compute node coordinates and draw them as scatter traces (Cytoscape/sigma/D3-force are far better). The code is not portable/adaptable, it is all-or-nothing whole-bundle inclusion, and its interaction design (modebar, chart hover) is chart-specific, not terminal-shaped. Crucially the marketing understates weight: the smallest partial bundle (plotly-basic) is ~1.0 MB and the full minified bundle is 3.5-4.6 MB, at odds with a lean zero-dependency SPA. We explicitly do NOT need market data, the only regime where its breadth would justify the weight. Reserve at most as an optional heavyweight chart-pane fallback for a specific statistical/3D chart; not useful for the terminal we are building.</reason>
<parameter name="license_confirmed">MIT (confirmed on plotly/plotly.js LICENSE; permissive, compatible)
key_files_or_ideas:
dist/ ships prebuilt UMD globals loadable with zero build (cdn.plot.ly/plotly-3.7.0.min.js). Partial bundles in dist/README.md: plotly-basic ~1.0 MB (bar/pie/scatter), plotly-finance ~1.2 MB (candlestick/OHLC), full 3.5-4.6 MB. No force-directed graph layout; network graphs require manual coordinate computation fed to scatter traces. Nothing reusable for command bar, fuzzy search, panes, or entity pages. Only conceivable use is a single optional chart pane, which is out of scope (no market data needed).</key_files_or_ideas>
</invoke>

name: D3.js
reason:
VERIFIED against live repo/CDN. License: ISC (permissive BSD-2-style, Copyright 2010-2023 Mike Bostock; LICENSE file confirmed) — no copyleft, fully safe to vendor. Activity: latest release v7.9.0 (Mar 2024); most recent main-branch commit May 28 2026 — mature/stable and still maintained (recent commits are docs/small fixes). No-build claim CONFIRMED: I fetched dist/d3.min.js and read its header — it is a single self-contained UMD file (`(t...).d3=t.d3||{}` wrapper) that exposes a `d3` global via a plain <script> tag, no npm/rollup/toolchain needed. Real sizes (measured, not marketing): full d3.min.js = 279,706 bytes (~273KB min); but modular dist files also ship prebuilt as UMD, so you can vendor JUST what a terminal needs — d3-force ~8KB + d3-selection ~13KB (+ d3-scale ~15KB) — instead of the whole bundle.

CAVEATS (adversarial): (1) D3 is a LIBRARY / substrate, not a frontend app — it contributes ZERO ready-made UI to the actual goals. Nothing here gives you a command bar, entity-and-function grammar, fuzzy search, tiling, or entity pages; it only serves the network-graph pane, and even there you write all the interaction (drag, zoom, linked-brushing) yourself on top of d3-force/d3-selection/d3-brush. (2) There is no 'interaction design' to study/port — it's a primitives API, so 'reference' value is nil; its value is purely as adopted code. (3) Strictly it does add a runtime dependency, so it violates a literal 'zero-dependency' reading — but it stays within 'no build step / no npm' since you drop one vendored JS file into the static SPA. SVG/canvas output carries no styling of its own, so nodes/edges inherit the existing skeleton+skin CSS cleanly. Net: adopt as the graph-viz substrate for the network pane only; do not expect it to move the command-bar/search/pane-layout work.
key_files_or_ideas: Vendor only the modular UMD dist files for the network-graph pane, not the 273KB full bundle: d3-force (dist/d3-force.min.js, ~8KB — forceSimulation/forceLink/forceManyBody/forceCenter for the entity graph), d3-selection (dist/d3-selection.min.js, ~13KB — data-join/enter-update-exit to bind persons/forums/evidence nodes to SVG), optionally d3-drag + d3-zoom (node dragging + pan/zoom), d3-brush (linked-brushing selection across panes), and d3-scale (~15KB) if you need any sizing/color scales. All ship as script-tag globals under the d3 namespace. Idea worth borrowing regardless of adoption: d3-force's velocity-Verlet force layout is the canonical, well-documented recipe for a self-organizing network graph — even a bespoke reimpl should follow its force model. Render nodes/edges as plain SVG elements so they inherit the project's skin CSS.

name: Frappe Charts
reason: Verified against the live repo (github.com/frappe/charts). MIT license confirmed. Zero-dependency claim is TRUE: package.json master has no dependencies/peerDependencies. No-build usable: ships prebuilt UMD bundle (dist/frappe-charts.min.umd.js) loadable via plain <script> from unpkg/jsdelivr (Rollup is only used to build the lib, not consume it) — fits our no-build/no-npm constraint exactly. Vanilla JS + SVG (96.6% JS), single small self-contained file, trivial to vendor. Activity is LOW/dormant (latest release v1.6.3; npm 1.6.2 ~5yrs old; last code commit Sep 2023; only 2024 commits are README chores; 'animate' feature self-deprecated as unmaintained) — but for a small stable SVG lib this is low-risk. CAVEAT on relevance: it solves NONE of our core terminal needs (no command bar/entity-function grammar, no fuzzy search, no tiled/linked panes, no entity pages, and no network/force-directed graph — our one genuinely-needed viz). It is purely a supporting chart component: line/bar/pie/percentage/heatmap. Adopt-worthy only because the GitHub-style contribution Heatmap is a strong, hard-to-replicate fit for activity/coverage panes and all charts are CSS-skinnable SVG that can be dropped in as-is. Not an interaction-design reference.
key_files_or_ideas: dist/frappe-charts.min.umd.js (self-contained UMD bundle, vendor directly, no build). Heatmap chart type = GitHub-style contribution calendar, ideal for activity/coverage panes. All chart types render skinnable SVG. Instantiate via new frappe.Chart(selector, {type, data, colors}). Not useful for the command bar, fuzzy search, pane tiling, or network graph — source those elsewhere.

name: ApexCharts.js
reason:

(1) RELEVANCE: ApexCharts is a charting library only — it provides NONE of the terminal interaction design the task judges (no command bar/entity-grammar, no fuzzy search, no tiled/linked panes, no entity pages) and no reusable vanilla-JS UI code. Critically, it CANNOT render a network/force-directed graph — the one visualization we actually need (that needs Cytoscape/sigma.js/vis-network/d3-force instead). Also violates the project's zero-dependency, no-build, no-npm constraint — it is itself a heavyweight SVG rendering dependency, not adaptable interaction code.</reason>

name: Highcharts
reason:
Verified against the live repo and EULA. Highcharts is source-available, NOT open source: free use is limited to personal (non-commercial, non-professional) and educational use only. The updated 2025/2026 EULA explicitly extends the paid-license requirement to internal business use, prototyping, R&D, and internal tools — so a commercial vc-brain product is license-incompatible; porting/translating its code is also not permitted. Beyond license, it's off-target: its real strength is Highstock financial time-series charting, which the task explicitly says we do NOT need, and it provides nothing for the actual asks (command-bar grammar, fuzzy search, tiled/linked panes, entity pages). Its networkgraph series is a proprietary chart module, not a reusable entity-graph surface. Repo is very active (~50k commits, TypeScript+SVG) and CDN-loadable with no build step, but that does not cure the license or relevance problems.</reason>

name: vis-network (vis.js)
reason: Verified against the live repo, not marketing. Dual Apache-2.0 OR MIT (both LICENSE files present; bundle header credits Almende B.V / visjs contributors) — fully permissive for vendoring. Actively maintained: v10.1.0 dated 2026-05-15 (date embedded in the downloaded bundle), ~3.6k stars, not archived. The no-build claim holds up under test: I downloaded the standalone UMD bundle (643,839 bytes = 632KB min / ~150KB gzip), a single self-contained file loadable via one <script> tag exposing vis.Network/vis.DataSet, with zero npm deps and no bundler. It drops straight into a vanilla static SPA as the entity-graph pane: physics layout, clustering, grouping, node/edge click+hover events wire cleanly into linked-pane navigation via `new vis.Network(container,{nodes,edges},options)`. Caveats (skeptical): 632KB is heavy for a zero-dep surface, though it's one vendored file adding no dependency graph; and it renders on canvas, so the graph is themed via vis-network's options API (colors/fonts) rather than inheriting the project's skeleton+skin CSS — you bridge theme tokens into options. Adopt (not adapt) because you vendor the library as-is; no code porting required. Best fit for the graph pane specifically, not the command bar / search / tiling, which are out of its scope.
license_confirmed: Apache-2.0 OR MIT (dual) — confirmed: repo ships LICENSE-APACHE-2.0 plus MIT text; standalone bundle header lists copyright Almende B.V (2011-2017) and visjs contributors (2017-2019). Permissive, compatible with vendoring into the static SPA.
key_files_or_ideas: Standalone no-build bundle: https://unpkg.com/vis-network@10.1.0/standalone/umd/vis-network.min.js (643,839 bytes; vendor this one file, do NOT use dist/ which needs peer deps). API surface for the graph pane: vis.DataSet for reactive nodes/edges; new vis.Network(container, {nodes, edges}, options); events network.on('click'|'hoverNode'|'selectNode', ...) to drive linked-pane navigation; options.groups + options.physics for clustering/layout; network.cluster*/openCluster for expand/collapse. Theming caveat: canvas-rendered, so map project skin tokens into options.nodes/edges/font colors rather than CSS. Scope: covers the network-graph visualization only — command bar, fuzzy search, and pane tiling are not provided by this library.

name: Sigma.js
reason: Verified via npm registry + tarball inspection (GitHub API was rate-limited). MIT-licensed and actively maintained (stable v3.0.3 published 2026-04-30, package modified 2026-06-09; v4 still alpha; 12.1k stars). Adversarial finding on the no-build claim: the candidate's caveat is HALF WRONG — sigma@3.0.3 DOES ship a real UMD bundle (dist/sigma.min.js, ~188KB, exposes global `Sigma` via `define.amd`/`module.exports`/`self.Sigma`), and both v3 and v4 official quickstarts present the CDN/script-tag path as a first-class option. BUT the core friction claim is correct and material: graphology is a MANDATORY separate runtime data library (sigma bundles only graphology-utils; I grepped the UMD and graphology core is not inside), so no-build adoption means loading TWO vendored UMD scripts (~280KB+: sigma ~188KB + graphology.umd.min) and constructing all graph data via graphology's API — genuinely more friction than a self-contained single-UMD lib (Cytoscape, vis-network). More decisively for fit: Sigma is a black-box WebGL rendering engine, not portable frontend code to translate, and it only pays off at tens of thousands of nodes. A persons/forums/briefs research corpus is almost certainly well below that scale, where Canvas/SVG suffices. It contributes nothing to the actual hard parts of this terminal (command-bar grammar, fuzzy search, tiled/linked panes, entity pages). Skip for the default zero-dependency build; keep as a VERIFIED scale-up escape hatch (UMD path is real and MIT-clean) only if the entity graph ever exceeds ~10k nodes.
use_as_detail: Overkill WebGL renderer that drags in a mandatory second library (graphology) for a corpus that won't reach WebGL scale; contributes nothing to the terminal's core interaction design.
license_confirmed: MIT (verified from npm package.json for sigma@3.0.3 and its required companion graphology@0.26.0)
key_files_or_ideas: Verified UMD build exists at sigma/dist/sigma.min.js (global `Sigma`, ~188KB min); requires graphology/dist/graphology.umd.min.js as a separate script. If scale ever demands WebGL, the two-script no-build path is real. Design ideas worth noting if hand-rolling a canvas graph pane instead: sigma's camera model, hover/highlight via node/edge 'reducers', and event-driven interaction API. Official quickstart: https://www.sigmajs.org/docs/quickstart/
zero_dep_compatible_detail: No-build-loadable via UMD, but not zero-dependency: mandatory graphology runtime library plus the sigma engine = two external vendored scripts.

name: AntV G6
reason: VERIFIED against the live repo/CDN. License: MIT (confirmed on repo). Activity: very active — latest release 5.1.1 dated 2026-04-17, 128 releases, ~12.2k stars; not stale. No-build usability: CONFIRMED REAL — a UMD bundle at https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/@antv/g6@5/dist/g6.min.js (also unpkg) loads via a plain <script> tag and exports the global window.G6; I fetched it and confirmed the standard UMD wrapper, and the official install docs list the script-tag path. So it drops into our zero-build vanilla static SPA as one external global — no npm/TS toolchain required. Size is the real trade-off: the min.js was still streaming past ~200KB when truncated; the full v5 bundle is ~1MB+ minified — a heavy single dependency that breaks the strict zero-dependency ethos, whereas the project deliberately ships no deps. Fit: G6 is a library you CONSUME, not a frontend codebase whose interaction design you port; it contributes nothing to the command-bar grammar, tiled/linked panes, or entity pages — only the entity network-graph pane. For that one pane it is genuinely strong: built-in force/dagre/circular layouts, edge/interaction behaviors, and graph analysis mean a polished graph explorer with little custom code. Verdict = adopt (MIT + no-build compatible, usable nearly as-is for the network pane), with the explicit caveat that it is heavy and that lighter alternatives (Cytoscape/vis-network) may suit a single terminal pane better; if minimal footprint is a hard requirement, downgrade to reference.
key_files_or_ideas: CDN UMD entry usable with no build step: https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/@antv/g6@5/dist/g6.min.js (or https://unpkg.com/@antv/g6@5/dist/g6.min.js), exposes global window.G6 (v5.1.1, MIT). Relevant surface = the entity network pane ONLY: instantiate new G6.Graph({container, layout, behaviors}); use built-in layouts (force/d3-force, dagre, circular, radial) for persons<->forums<->debates<->evidence links, plus interaction behaviors (drag-canvas, zoom-canvas, activate-relations, hover/click states) and combo/edge-bundling for clustered entities. Wire node/edge click to your linked-pane routing (open entity page) rather than G6's own UI. Does NOT provide command-bar, fuzzy search, tiled panes, or entity-page layout — pair with a separate solution for those. Weight caveat: ~1MB+ minified UMD; if that violates the zero-dep budget, prefer a lighter graph lib and use G6 only as a design reference for layout/behavior UX.

name: force-graph / 3d-force-graph (vasturiano)
reason: VERIFIED, claims hold up. LICENSE: MIT confirmed from the actual LICENSE file (Copyright 2018 Vasco Asturiano) and npm registry `license: MIT` — fully permissive, adoptable/vendorable. ACTIVITY: genuinely active, not stale marketing — latest v1.51.4 published 2026-04-16 (~3mo before today), regular cadence into 2026 (1.51.1 Feb, 1.51.2 Mar, 1.51.3+1.51.4 Apr), 197 published versions since 2018, ~2.1k stars. NO-BUILD (the load-bearing question): true for the CONSUMER despite one caveat — the repo itself builds with rollup+babel, but the DISTRIBUTED artifact is a prebuilt UMD bundle `dist/force-graph.min.js`. I downloaded it and confirmed: UMD wrapper that exposes a global `ForceGraph` (`(t=globalThis).ForceGraph=n()`), so `<script src>` + `ForceGraph()(el).graphData(...)` works with zero build, matching our vanilla static SPA exactly. SELF-CONTAINED: verified the bundle has zero `require()` externals, zero `THREE` references, and inlines the d3-force sim (`alphaTarget`/`velocityDecay`) + lodash + tween — so although the package lists ~14 transitive npm deps, the drop-in file pulls NOTHING at runtime. This is the key correction to the "zero-dependency" concern: you vendor ONE file, not a dep tree. REAL SIZE: single file 177.6 KB minified / 57.6 KB gzipped (not the 6.46 MB npm `unpackedSize`, which includes src+examples+mjs — that figure would be misleading). Canvas-2D confirmed (`getContext`, no WebGL/three needed). FIT: exactly right scope for our entity-network pane — renderer + physics with onNodeClick/onNodeHover/zoom/pan for click-to-link between tiled panes; the candidate is honest that it's a renderer not an analysis lib (no graph algorithms like Cytoscape), which is fine since we don't need centrality/pathfinding. SCOPE CAVEAT: this covers only the graph-visualization slice of the terminal — it contributes nothing to the command bar, fuzzy search, tiled panes, or entity pages. Adopt it as the graph component; source those elsewhere. (If 3D were ever wanted, the sibling `3d-force-graph` additionally requires three.js as an external global — heavier, avoid unless needed; the 2D `force-graph` is the clean self-contained pick.)
key_files_or_ideas: Drop-in file: dist/force-graph.min.js (UMD, exposes global `ForceGraph`, ~178KB min / 58KB gz, self-contained). CDN: https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/force-graph or unpkg. Core API to wire into pane-linking: ForceGraph()(domEl).graphData({nodes,links}).nodeId().nodeLabel().nodeColor().linkColor().onNodeClick(node=>openEntityPane(node)).onNodeHover().nodeCanvasObject() for custom node rendering (terminal-style labels), .zoom()/.centerAt() for pane focus sync. Vendor the MIT license header (already embedded at top of the min.js). Ignore the npm dep list — the UMD bundle inlines everything.
license_confirmed: MIT (verified from repo LICENSE file, Copyright (c) 2018 Vasco Asturiano; matches npm registry and embedded header in dist/force-graph.min.js)

name: Fuse.js
reason: Verified on live repo/CDN: Apache-2.0, genuinely zero-dependency, actively maintained (v7.5.0 released 2026-07-13, ~20.4k stars, not archived), ~25KB min. Directly satisfies the 'universal fuzzy search' requirement with weighted multi-field ranking + threshold tuning, all client-side, and pairs naturally with the command bar (pipe query text through Fuse to rank entity matches). Runs with no build step in the static SPA. IMPORTANT CORRECTION to the candidate pitch: it claims a UMD global (window.Fuse) via plain <script> — that is outdated. v7.5.0 dist/ ships ONLY ESM (.mjs), CJS (.cjs), and worker builds; there is no UMD build and no window.Fuse global. It remains no-build compatible via <script type=module> import Fuse from 'https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/fuse.js@7.5.0/dist/fuse.min.mjs'. Note: this is a search engine, not UI/interaction design — it provides no command-bar/pane/graph code to reference, only the ranking core.
key_files_or_ideas: Load via ESM (no build): import Fuse from 'https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/fuse.js@7.5.0/dist/fuse.min.mjs' (NOT a UMD global — that claim is stale). Build one in-memory Fuse index per entity type or a unified index with a 'type' field; use weighted keys to boost name/title over body/description for command-bar entity ranking; expose threshold/distance for tuning. dist files: fuse.min.mjs (~25KB), fuse.basic.min.mjs (~19KB), fuse.worker.min.mjs for off-main-thread indexing of larger corpora.

name: fzf-for-js
reason: Verified: BSD-3-Clause (permissive, vendorable with attribution), ZERO runtime dependencies (the 27 deps are all devDependencies for building the lib/docs), and it ships prebuilt ESM (dist/fzf.es.js) + UMD via the package `module`/`exports` fields — so it drops into a zero-build vanilla-JS static SPA either by vendoring the single dist file or importing from jsDelivr. It is a headless fuzzy-MATCHING/ranking library, NOT a frontend: the repo's only UI is a React/Tailwind/MDX docs demo, so there is no command bar, tiled panes, entity page, or graph viz to reuse. Its value is exactly the fzf terminal ranking model (v1/v2, prefix/word-boundary/camelCase bonuses, tiebreaks) plus `.positions` for match highlighting — a strong thematic fit for the command-bar 'entity + function' search and universal fuzzy search. Caveat: effectively frozen (last release v0.5.2, 2023-04-25, ~3yr stale), but low-risk because it is a small self-contained algorithm port with no supply-chain surface (~2.4M weekly npm downloads, ~954 stars). Verdict is 'adopt' for the search-ranking slice only; it does not cover the terminal UI/layout/graph work.
key_files_or_ideas: Load-bearing pieces: (1) prebuilt dist/fzf.es.js — self-contained ESM, zero runtime deps, vendor it or import from https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/fzf@0.5.2/dist/fzf.es.js; no build step needed. (2) API: `new Fzf(list, { selector, tiebreakers, casing, fuzzy: 'v1'|'v2'|false })` then `.find(query)` returns entries with `.score`, `.item`, and `.positions` (Set of matched char indices for highlighting). (3) The reusable IDEA is fzf's ranking model itself — prefix bonus, word/camelCase boundary bonus, consecutive-match bonus, and tiebreak ordering — which gives the 'terminal fuzzy finder' feel for the command grammar; alternative to Fuse.js when you want fzf-style scoring rather than weighted-field search. No UI/interaction code is reusable (docs demo is React/Tailwind, irrelevant to the vanilla SPA).

name: microfuzz
reason: Verified live repo. MIT license confirmed (LICENSE shipped in package `files`). Last commit May 22, 2025; ~260 stars, v1.0.0 — low-volume but maintained, not dead. Genuinely relevant: it is purpose-built for autocomplete/jump-to/command-palette and returns ranked results with character-level highlight ranges — exactly the primitive the command bar and universal fuzzy search need, and a lighter alternative to Fuse.js. Zero runtime dependencies (all tooling is devDeps). BUT the candidate's "no build required" claim is false: src/index.js is Flow-typed (`// @flow`, generic type syntax) and needs transpiling, and the published dist/index.js is CommonJS (`exports.__esModule`, `require("./normalizeText")`, delegates to `./impl`) split across 2-3 modules with no ESM/`type:module` field — so it will not drop into a no-build browser `<script type=module>` without a bundler. It has no frontend/UI/interaction code at all; it is a pure matching function. Because the algorithm is tiny and zero-dep, the practical route for a no-build vanilla-ESM SPA is to vendor it: strip the Flow annotations and inline the impl/normalizeText helpers into one ESM file. That is porting, hence adapt not adopt.
key_files_or_ideas: Public API createFuzzySearch(list, opts) -> query -> [{item, score, matches: Range[]}] and fuzzyMatch; core idea = ordered-subsequence (non-consecutive, no transposition) matching + simple heuristic ranking + returned highlight ranges for rendering matched chars in the command palette; diacritics/case-insensitive normalization (src/normalizeText.js). Repo source files to port: src/index.js, src/impl.js, src/normalizeText.js. Performance cited: ~7ms first / <1.5ms subsequent search over ~4500 short labels, in-memory no index — fine for per-keystroke universal search.

name: FlexSearch / MiniSearch
reason: Verified against actual repos. Both are permissively licensed, zero-transitive-dependency, in-memory full-text search libraries that ship prebuilt browser bundles (UMD + ESM) on jsdelivr — usable via a single vendored <script>/ESM import with NO build step and NO npm, so they satisfy the project's zero-build constraint. FlexSearch: Apache-2.0 (LICENSE confirmed), latest 0.8.212, release v0.8.2 May 2025, ~13.8k stars, actively maintained. MiniSearch: MIT (LICENSE confirmed), latest 7.2.0 published Sept 16 2025, zero external deps, ~6k stars, actively maintained (repo uses no GitHub Releases page — not a staleness signal). CAVEAT: neither is a frontend — there is no UI, command bar, pane layout, or interaction design to port or study; they are headless search backends. Verdict is 'adopt' the LIBRARY as the document-search engine behind the universal search bar (MiniSearch preferred for fuzzy+prefix+field-boosting over long brief/evidence prose; FlexSearch if corpus scales large). Pair with Fuse.js/fzf for the command-bar entity+function grammar, since these excel at document full-text, not short-string command matching. FlexSearch's '1,000,000x faster' marketing is puffery and irrelevant at this corpus size.
license_confirmed: Apache-2.0 (FlexSearch — verified from LICENSE file) / MIT (MiniSearch — verified from LICENSE file). Both permissive and compatible with adoption/redistribution.
key_files_or_ideas: Adopt as a headless library, not code to port. MiniSearch: vendor dist/umd/index.js (or ESM dist/es/index.js) from CDN; API = new MiniSearch({fields:[...], storeFields:[...]}), .addAll(docs), .search(query, {fuzzy:0.2, prefix:true, boost:{...}}) — ideal for tokenized full-text over brief/evidence text with field boosting and typo tolerance. FlexSearch: dist/flexsearch.bundle.min.js UMD; Document index for multi-field, faster/heavier, use only if corpus grows large. Neither provides UI — build the terminal command bar, panes, and network graph separately, and use Fuse.js/fzf for the 'ENTITY FUNCTION' command grammar. Recommended pairing: MiniSearch for universal document search + a lightweight fuzzy matcher for command tokens.

name: FDC3 (finos/FDC3) — the open standard
reason: Verified real and very active (v2.2.3 released May 14 2026; code packages at 3.0.0-alpha; ~5,100+ commits). Candidate's license claim is partly inaccurate: the CODE packages (fdc3-context, fdc3-schema, fdc3-standard, workbench) are confirmed Apache-2.0, but the spec prose is under the Community Specification License 1.0, NOT CC-BY-4.0, and there is no root LICENSE file. The ONLY actual frontend is the FDC3 Workbench, which is a React + Vite + Material-UI v4 + MobX app requiring a full build step (npm run dev) — incompatible with our zero-dependency, no-build vanilla-JS SPA, and too framework-heavy to port. FDC3 is a cross-application desktop interop wire protocol, not a search-terminal UI; its runtime is irrelevant to a single static SPA and it is market-data-oriented. The genuine value is conceptual: the colored User-Channel model is the formalized Bloomberg pane-linking pattern (map to a per-pane color-channel selector + per-channel current-context fan-out), Intents+resolver map onto an entity+function command grammar, and a handful of Apache-2.0 JSON Schema context nouns could be vendored as vocabulary. That is design/spec worth studying, not adoptable or adaptable code.
license_confirmed: Apache-2.0 for code/schema packages (confirmed via @finos/fdc3-context and @finos/fdc3-schema package.json license field); spec/standard prose is Community Specification License 1.0 — NOT CC-BY-4.0 as the candidate claimed; no root LICENSE file (404).
key_files_or_ideas: Ideas worth adopting (not code): (1) Colored User Channels — joinUserChannel(channelId)/broadcast(context)/addContextListener() → model tiled/linked panes as a per-pane color selector with a per-channel current-context and fan-out to co-channeled panes only. (2) Intents + resolver — raiseIntent('ViewProfile', {type:'fdc3.instrument',...}) → map DES/GP/CN/GRAPH functions to intent names routed by a resolver, giving the 'ENTITY FUNCTION' command grammar. (3) Vendorable vocabulary: Apache-2.0 JSON Schema context definitions in packages/fdc3-context and packages/fdc3-schema. Not reusable: toolbox/fdc3-workbench (React+Vite+MUI+MobX, build-step required).

name: @finos/fdc3 (npm library)
reason:
Verified via npm registry API + web search. License Apache-2.0 (permissive, vendorable with attribution); actively maintained (latest 2.2.3 published 2026-05-14, 3.0.0-alpha.2 June 2026; repo finos/FDC3 not archived). BUT this package contains NO frontend code whatsoever — no command bar, search, graph, CSS, or SPA. It is a pure TypeScript-types + JSON-Schema + helper layer (getAgent/fdc3Ready). The npm package is also a meta-package pulling 4 @finos sub-deps and ships as ESM requiring a bundler, so it is not drop-in for a zero-dep no-build app. Its context vocabulary (Instrument, Position, Portfolio) is market/finance-desktop-oriented and a poor fit for our persons/forums/evidence/briefs corpus, which explicitly needs no market data. The candidate's 'directly vendorable vocabulary' claim is overstated. The only genuinely valuable, transferable asset is the interaction-design SPEC: FDC3's Channels + broadcast(context) + intent-resolution model is a clean, well-specified pattern for a linked-pane context bus (Bloomberg-style linked-group coloring) that maps to our tiled/linked-panes requirement. That is a design reference to study, not code to adopt or port. Hence: reference.</reason>
<parameter name="license_confirmed">Apache-2.0
key_files_or_ideas:
IDEA (the only reusable part): FDC3 "Channels" model as a pane-linking bus — apps join a channel and broadcast/listen for Context objects; user/app/private channels give Bloomberg-style linked-group semantics. Study the spec at fdc3.finos.org/docs/api/spec (Channels, broadcast/addContextListener) and intent-resolution (raiseIntent/IntentResolution) to design our own {type,id,payload} context bus for linked panes. The versioned JSON Schemas at fdc3.finos.org/schemas/2.2/context/ show a clean pattern for typed entity schemas, but the actual context types (Instrument/Position/Portfolio) are market-oriented and should be replaced wholesale with our own person/forum/evidence/brief types. Do NOT npm-install the package (4 @finos deps, ESM, needs a bundler) — at most hand-copy the structural pattern. No frontend/UI code exists to lift.</parameter>
</invoke>

name: Perspective (finos/perspective)
reason:
Verified against the live repo/npm/docs. License Apache-2.0 (clean) and it IS technically no-build usable — <perspective-viewer> is a real framework-agnostic Custom Element with a purpose-built dist/cdn ESM bundle importable from jsdelivr in a plain HTML file. But it fails our purpose on three hard grounds. (1) NOT zero-dependency: the WASM payload is multi-MB (perspective-server.wasm 2.28 MB, perspective-viewer.wasm 921 kB, perspective-js.wasm 217 kB) plus worker assets — the opposite of our skeleton+skin ethos. (2) NOTHING to adapt: the frontend is compiled Rust->WASM (Rust 34%/C++ 29%), not portable JS; you can only embed the black-box binary, so 'adapt' is impossible. (3) Wrong tool: it's a tabular streaming-analytics grid + d3fc STATISTICAL charts (bar/line/scatter/heatmap/treemap/sunburst) with NO node-link/network graph, and it covers none of our actual needs — command-bar grammar, universal fuzzy search, linked-pane shell, entity pages, network viz. Our corpus is a small qualitative entity graph with explicitly no market/large-tabular data, i.e. the exact workload Perspective exists for is absent. Also stale metadata: candidate says v3.x but current is v4.5.2 (Jul 2026), and the project moved from FINOS to the OpenJS Foundation (Oct 2025), so the 'finos/perspective' name/namespace is drifting. Only marginal value: its FDC3-style context-linking pattern is worth noting as design inspiration for linked panes, but that's an FDC3 idea, not Perspective code.</reason>
<parameter name="license_confirmed">Apache-2.0
key_files_or_ideas: Custom Element <perspective-viewer> loaded via dist/cdn/perspective-viewer.js (320 kB) + dist/cdn/perspective.js (231 kB), which then pull dist/wasm binaries: perspective-server.wasm 2.28 MB, perspective-viewer.wasm 921 kB, perspective-js.wasm 217 kB. Frontend is compiled Rust->WASM (not portable JS). Only transferable IDEA: FDC3-style context-linking (retarget a pane's view when its channel/context changes) as inspiration for our tiled/linked panes; its config-driven filter/pivot toolbar is a minor reference for a data-detail pane, but we have no tabular data to pivot and it offers no network-graph, command bar, or fuzzy search.

name: regular-table (finos/regular-table)
reason: Verified against the live repo. Apache-2.0 (package.json + GitHub). Actively maintained: last commit 2026-07-09 with steady monthly commits through 2026. Zero runtime dependencies confirmed (empty dependencies object; all 13 packages are devDeps). Consumer needs NO build step: npm ships a prebuilt ESM bundle at dist/esm/regular-table.js = 23,774 bytes (~23KB), loadable via <script type=module> from CDN or vendored as one file; the repo's esbuild/build.mjs only matters if you fork the TS source. Styling is plain CSS (dist/css/*.css, 0.4-2.6KB each; LESS is pre-compiled in dist). This is a near-exact fit for a zero-dependency vanilla-JS static SPA with a skin system. Scope caveat: it is a virtualized-table primitive ONLY — it solves the large lazily-loaded tables (evidence-signal lists, attendance rosters, brief tables) via its async DataListener virtual data model, and your skin CSS styles <regular-table> directly. It does NOT provide the command bar, fuzzy search, tiled/linked panes, or network graph; the candidate blurb's FDC3 pairing is glue you write yourself. Minor: shipped material.css @imports Open Sans from Google Fonts — strip that one line for a fully offline SPA. Adopt for the tables slice.
key_files_or_ideas: dist/esm/regular-table.js (~23KB prebuilt ESM Custom Element, registers <regular-table> as an import side-effect — vendor this one file, no build). dist/css/container.css + material.css + sub-cell-scrolling.css (plain-CSS skin starting points). Core API: setDataListener(fn) + draw(), where fn returns only the visible viewport slice — ideal for lazily paging rows from the markdown corpus. Reference examples: examples/spreadsheet.md, examples/two_billion_rows, examples/file_browser.

name: FDC3 Sail (finos/FDC3-Sail)
reason: Verified against the live repo: Apache-2.0, active (v2.0.0 ~Mar 2026, not archived), but a React app requiring a mandatory npm build (`npm install && npm run build && npm start`, no pre-built static dist) — fundamentally incompatible with vc-brain's zero-dependency, no-build vanilla-JS SPA. Frontend is not separable and nothing drops in. Domain mismatch too: FDC3 Sail solves cross-app FDC3 interop (channel joining, context broadcast, intent-to-app resolution), NOT a command-bar grammar, fuzzy search, entity pages, or network graphs. The only transferable value is the design pattern of a color-channel picker + context-broadcast bus as an analog for Bloomberg-style "linked panes" — but that's a trivial pub/sub concept not worth porting from React/TS. Marketing framing as a "command/intent resolver blueprint" overstates it (its resolver picks apps, not commands). Study the linked-pane UI idea; adopt/adapt nothing.
key_files_or_ideas: Ideas only (no code portable to zero-dep vanilla SPA): (1) color-channel picker UI + context-broadcast fan-out as a design analog for Bloomberg-style linked/color-grouped panes; (2) intent-resolver popup pattern (though it resolves apps, not command-bar functions). Repo is React/Vite/TS with a required build; verified via github.com/finos/FDC3-Sail landing page and /blob/main/README.md. Not production-ready per its own README.

name: fdc3-desktop-agent (finos/fdc3-desktop-agent, orig. nkolba/desktop-agent)
reason: Adversarial check confirms: Apache-2.0 (verified in repo + package.json name "desktop-agent" v0.0.2), but the repo was ARCHIVED Oct 3, 2023, read-only, explicitly superseded by FDC3 Sail, with an archive-banner warning of possible bugs/security vulns. It is a Chrome Extension (MV) with a webpack 4 + TypeScript 3.8 build step (npm i && npm run build) — NOT no-build compatible and structurally alien to a zero-dep static SPA (background service worker + content script + injected api.ts + manifest + popup). The candidate's "wired over postMessage" claim is inaccurate: api.ts (~250 lines) uses DOM CustomEvents relayed by the content script to the background worker, not window.postMessage between frames — so for iframe/pane linking you'd translate, not lift. The one durable value is design, not code: system-channels.ts defines colored user channels (red/orange/yellow/green/blue/purple/global with displayMetadata color+name) that map cleanly onto Bloomberg's colored linking groups, and broadcast/addContextListener is the linked-pane context-propagation model. But that pattern is better sourced from the FDC3 standard (finos/FDC3) directly; this archived impl adds nothing adoptable. Net: study it for the linked-pane / color-channel concept, port nothing.
key_files_or_ideas: Ideas worth studying: (1) FDC3 user-channel model = Bloomberg colored linking groups — see src/system-channels.ts (red/orange/yellow/green/blue/purple/global, each with displayMetadata {color, color2, name}); (2) broadcast + addContextListener as the linked-pane context-propagation contract (src/api.ts, ~250 lines, uses DOM CustomEvents); (3) the color-channel picker UI concept in popup.html/popup.js. For your SPA, re-implement channel linking with window.postMessage or BroadcastChannel across iframes/panes rather than porting the extension's content-script/background relay. Canonical spec source: finos/FDC3 (the standard) rather than this archived agent.

name: fdc3-web (morganstanley/fdc3-web)
reason: Verified: Apache-2.0 (repo page + ui-provider/package.json), active (v0.15.0 ~June 2026) but tiny adoption (~6 stars). Technically a poor fit for a zero-dep no-build vanilla-JS SPA: the only relevant frontend is the ui-provider package, which is Lit/lit-element WEB COMPONENTS in TypeScript (hard deps lit ^3.3.3, lit-element ^4.2.2) requiring TS compilation AND the Lit runtime — violating our zero-dependency/no-build constraint. The channel-selector.component.ts (~280 lines) and app-resolver.component.ts are tightly coupled to FDC3 abstractions (DesktopAgent, Channel, getCurrentChannel/joinUserChannel/leaveCurrentChannel, userChannelChanged). Cannot adopt as-is. Not worth 'adapt' either: porting means deleting Lit + FDC3 and keeping only a small themeable CSS block and a trivial state machine — cheaper to rewrite in vanilla JS. Real value is conceptual: the FDC3 colored user-channel linking model (join a color, broadcast context, co-channeled panes retarget) is exactly the Bloomberg 'linked groups' pattern for our tiled/linked panes, and the picker's hover-expand colored-dot UI (CSS-custom-property themed, verified @keyframes show-channel-name) is a useful visual reference. But that model is the FINOS FDC3 STANDARD, not invented here — for same-origin panes we replace all its cross-origin/iframe messaging machinery (WCP/postMessage) with ~20 lines of vanilla pub/sub or BroadcastChannel. Study the design; don't take the code.
key_files_or_ideas: Ideas worth studying (not code to import): (1) FDC3 colored user-channel linking model = join a color -> broadcast selected entity (person/forum/brief) -> co-channeled panes retarget; maps directly to our tiled/linked-pane 'link group' feature. (2) UI treatment in projects/ui-provider/src/channel-selector.component.ts (~280 lines, Lit): hover-expand colored-dot channel picker, themed entirely via CSS custom properties (--ms-channel-btn-*, @keyframes show-channel-name) — reimplement in vanilla JS/CSS. (3) projects/ui-provider/src/app-resolver.component.ts = intent-resolver chrome pattern (pick which handler for an action) — analogous to our command-bar function grammar disambiguation. Canonical spec reference for the linking model is FINOS FDC3 (finos/FDC3), which this repo implements. For our single-origin SPA, replace the messaging-provider/WCP postMessage transport with a ~20-line in-memory pub/sub or BroadcastChannel; skip the desktop-agent/app-directory machinery entirely.

name: desktopJS (morganstanley/desktopJS)
reason: Verified against the live repo: Apache-2.0, actively maintained (last commit 2026-07-01, latest release v4.0.2), TypeScript bundled with rollup and published as npm package @morgan-stanley/desktopjs — so it requires a build step and carries dependencies, incompatible with a zero-dep no-build vanilla SPA. More importantly it is not a UI/read-surface candidate at all: it is a desktop-container abstraction (Electron/OpenFin/browser) normalizing OS-level window management, tray, notifications, and a cross-container MessageBus. It provides none of the target primitives (command-bar grammar, fuzzy search, tiled/linked in-page panes, entity pages, network graph). Its MessageBus is heavyweight cross-window RPC at the OS-container layer, not in-page pane linking (which is a trivial ~20-line pub/sub in a browser SPA), and its 'window grouping' is OS-window tabbing, not tiled DOM panes. The candidate's own note concedes it is 'Not a UI/visual candidate.' Its only conceivable value is a speculative future Electron/multi-container build with portable pane messaging — out of scope for a browser-only read surface — so even as a design reference it is marginal. Judged on the stated criterion (adaptable frontend code or interaction design), it fails.
key_files_or_ideas: Only tangential idea: the MessageBus pub/sub abstraction and window-grouping model as a portable messaging concept IF a future Electron/OpenFin build is ever pursued. Not applicable to the current browser-only zero-dep SPA, where linked-pane messaging is a trivial in-page event bus. No reusable frontend/visual code (no command bar, fuzzy search, tiled panes, entity pages, or graph).

name: Glue42 Core (Glue42/core, now interop.io io.Connect)
reason: Adversarial verification: the stated repo URL https://github.com/Glue42/core is DEAD — HTTP 404 on the landing page and on raw master/main files; the Glue42 org still exists (200) but the core repo was deleted/made private and development moved to the commercial interop.io (InteropIO). Source survives only in frozen ~2022 third-party forks. License IS MIT (confirmed via forks' package.json and the announcement blog), so licensing isn't the blocker — but the code is technically incompatible with our project. Forks show a lerna monorepo (workspaces packages/*) built with lerna+gulp+rollup+TypeScript and consumed as npm packages (@glue42/web, @glue42/web-platform, @glue42/fdc3, @glue42/gateway-ws) — the exact opposite of our zero-dependency no-build vanilla-JS SPA. It also solves the wrong problem: it's a cross-APPLICATION interop bus (postMessage/WebSocket/Web-Worker + FDC3 method/window management) so multiple standalone web apps interoperate across windows; our linked panes live inside one SPA and need ~20 lines of in-app pub/sub, not an interop gateway. Core ships no terminal UI, command bar, search, entity pages, or graph. The only genuinely relevant thing is the Channels/Linker colored-group interaction concept (the Bloomberg colored-Group linking metaphor) and the FDC3 channel model — worth studying as design inspiration for a colored linked-pane selector, but that's a documented interaction pattern (better described in interop.io docs than in reusable code), not importable frontend. Hence reference, not adopt/adapt.

Implement in our SPA as a tiny vanilla pub/sub: a channel registry keyed by color, panes join a color, selecting an entity broadcasts {type,id} to same-color panes. The interop.io Bloomberg-adapter docs are the clearest public description of Bloomberg colored Groups <-> channel mapping. Do NOT pull in @glue42/web or @glue42/fdc3 packages. Evidence: fork package.json at raw.githubusercontent.com/valentin-aleksandrov/core/master/package.json (lerna/gulp/rollup/TS, MIT); original https://github.com/Glue42/core = 404.

name: Dockview (mathuo/dockview)
reason: Verified against the real repo/npm, not marketing. dockview-core@7.0.2 is MIT (triple-confirmed: npm metadata, bundle @license header, repo) with ZERO runtime and ZERO peer dependencies. The shipped ESM build (dist/package/main.esm.mjs) is a single self-contained ~807KB file with zero import statements and one export block — it loads directly via <script type=module> from a CDN or vendored copy with NO build step, NO npm, and NO TS compile on our side, which fits our no-build vanilla SPA. The one browser hazard (process.env) is explicitly typeof-guarded for plain-browser contexts per the maintainer's own comment. Add one CSS file (~124KB) plus JS-exported themes. Genuinely active: v7.0.2 released 2026-06-22, 3.3k stars, 87% TS. HONEST SCOPE: it solves only the pane geometry (split/tab/group/float/pop-out + layout serialize/deserialize) — exactly our tiled-panes need — but the colored linked-channel bus, command-bar grammar, fuzzy search, entity pages, and network graph are all still ours to build. We consume the compiled lib as-is (no code porting), so it's a near-drop-in adopt for the panes shell layer, not the whole terminal.
key_files_or_ideas: Consume the prebuilt package (do NOT vendor the TS source): dockview-core@7.0.2 dist/package/main.esm.mjs (~807KB, single self-contained ESM, browser-loadable) + dist/styles/dockview.css (~124KB). Load via CDN https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/dockview-core@7.0.2/dist/package/main.esm.mjs or vendor the two files locally. Core API: createDockview(element, {createComponent}) / DockviewComponent; addPanel({id, component}); toJSON()/fromJSON() for layout serialize-deserialize (drives our saved workspace layouts). Built-in themes exported from JS (themeDark/themeLight/themeAbyss/themeDracula/etc.) plus applyTabGroupAccent for Bloomberg-style colored tab groups. Design idea to reuse even if not adopted: its group/panel model + drag-drop overlay + pop-out-to-window pattern for the multi-monitor terminal shell. NOT provided (build ourselves): command bar, fuzzy search, entity pages, network graph, and the cross-pane linked color-channel bus.

name: Golden Layout (golden-layout/golden-layout)
reason: Verified MIT license (repo sidebar + shipped LICENSE), zero runtime dependencies (no deps/peerDeps in package.json). Repo is maintained but frozen: last real release v2.6.0 was Sep 26, 2022; commits since (through Jan 25, 2026) are docs-only. Technically no-build-compatible but NOT a clean drop-in: the published 2.6.0 ships a multi-file pre-compiled ESM tree (dist/esm/index.js, native-ESM loadable, relative imports) + CSS themes, but NO single-file bundle — the dist/bundle/*.min.js path 404s and issue #845 confirms users complaining build:bundles is broken. So no-build use means vendoring the dist/esm folder + goldenlayout-base.css + a theme, or a CDN import. It solves ONLY the tiled/linked pane layer (drag-to-split, tab-stacking, pop-out, serializable LayoutConfig) — nothing for the command bar, fuzzy search, entity pages, or network graph. Direct-DOM, opinionated component-registration lifecycle that is harder to sync with reactive state and heavier than the project's hand-rolled skeleton+skin ethos; it is the explicit dated fallback behind Dockview. Best value for a zero-dep vanilla SPA is porting its proven pane-interaction model into a lean in-house manager; wholesale adoption is a viable fallback only if a batteries-included docking engine is wanted over Dockview.
key_files_or_ideas: Pane-management layer only. Interaction model to study/port: drag-to-split, tab-stacking, floating pop-out windows, and serializable LayoutConfig JSON for save/restore of tiled layouts — exactly the terminal multi-pane pattern. Reusable assets if adopting wholesale: prebuilt dist/esm/ ES-module tree (native-browser loadable, zero runtime deps) + dist/css/goldenlayout-base.css and a theme (goldenlayout-dark-theme.css / light). Caveat: no single-file bundle in npm 2.6.0 (issue #845); must vendor the multi-file esm tree or self-build build:bundles. Component-registration lifecycle is opinionated and direct-DOM, so wiring it to reactive entity state needs a manual bridge.

name: d3fc (d3fc/d3fc)
reason: Verified: MIT license (clean, permissive), but it fails the project's core constraints. (1) NOT zero-dependency — the UMD bundle (build/d3fc.js, ~387KB, verified on unpkg) hard-requires 12 separate d3-* modules (d3-array/scale/time/selection/shape/brush/zoom/etc.); documented usage loads full D3 first, so real footprint is ~270KB D3 + ~387KB d3fc ≈ 650KB+ of third-party JS, contradicting the zero-dependency vanilla-JS mandate. It IS script-tag-loadable (no build required), so no-build is technically satisfiable, but only by adopting D3 wholesale. (2) Wrong problem domain — it is a financial time-series charting lib (candlestick/OHLC/line/series + technical indicators); the task explicitly says market data is NOT needed and the corpus (persons/forums/debates/signals) has little to chart. (3) Zero coverage of the actual terminal surfaces: no command-bar grammar, no fuzzy search, no tiled/linked panes, no entity pages. (4) Does NOT do the required network-graph visualization (force-directed graphs are d3-force, a different lib). (5) Maintenance claim overstated — last commit and last release both 2024-07-30, ~2 years stale as of 2026-07. Candidate's 'complement for chart panes' pitch is the only plausible use and it is marginal versus writing lightweight custom SVG.
key_files_or_ideas: Prebuilt UMD: build/d3fc.js (~387KB, unpkg) — usable via script tag but requires a global D3 (12 d3-* modules). A-la-carte ESM packages (@d3fc/d3fc-series, @d3fc/d3fc-technical-indicator, @d3fc/d3fc-webgl, @d3fc/d3fc-chart) exist but are npm/bundler-only, not usable in a no-build vanilla SPA. Only marginally relevant idea: composable cartesian 'series' API for signal timelines — but easily replaced by hand-rolled SVG given zero-dep constraint. Nothing here informs command-bar grammar, fuzzy search, linked panes, or network graphs.

name: OpenFin Workspace / HERE Core starter (built-on-openfin/workspace-starter)

name: uFuzzy
key_files_or_ideas: dist/uFuzzy.iife.min.js (single-<script> IIFE, global `var uFuzzy`, self-contained, no import/require — verified by reading the raw file). API surface: uFuzzy().search(haystack, needle) returns [idxs, info, order]; info exposes per-match stats (chars, terms, interLft2/interLft1=word-boundary starts, start, intraIns, interIns, cases) plus ranges for highlighting; the default `sort` is a plain comparator over those stats you can override — this is the transparent, tunable ranking to prioritize entity vs. function matches in the command bar. f.highlight(str, ranges) emits <mark> spans for autocomplete rendering. latinize() + permute() handle accents and out-of-order tokens. Use as the matcher behind universal search + command-bar autocomplete; build all UI/pane/grammar yourself.

name: fzy.js
reason: Verified against the actual repo (jhawthorn/fzy.js). MIT license (confirmed in package.json + LICENSE), 163 stars, last commit 2024-09-11 (maintenance only; low-churn canonical port, not archived). The real asset is a single ES6-module index.js of 195 lines with ZERO external dependencies (no import/require, no Node globals) — runs directly in the browser via <script type="module"> or a bare import. The rollup 'build' script only emits a CommonJS variant for npm's main field and is irrelevant to browser use; devDeps (rollup/jest) are never shipped. It is NOT frontend/UI or interaction design — it's a pure scoring primitive exposing score(needle,haystack), positions(needle,haystack) (matched-char indices, good for highlighting), and hasMatch(). It favors consecutive letters and word/path-segment starts, so acronym-style command-bar queries score well — exactly the fzf-style scoring the brief names. Drop-in for the zero-dep vanilla-JS SPA: vendor index.js, import, and wrap with your own filter loop; if they avoid ES modules, deleting the single trailing export line to attach to a global is a trivial edit. Skeptical note: brief's '~1-2KB' understates it (195 lines ≈ ~5KB unminified), but every other claim (MIT, zero deps, single file, no build, browser-usable) checks out.

Exports: score(needle,haystack) for ranking, positions(needle,haystack) for match-highlighting in the command bar/search results, hasMatch(needle,haystack) as a cheap prefilter, plus SCORE_* constants. Usage pattern: filter candidates with hasMatch, sort by score desc, render with positions() to bold matched chars. example.js shows the filter+sort+highlight loop. name: quick-score
reason: Verified against the actual repo + published package. MIT-licensed (LICENSE file, John Dunning 2022), zero runtime/peer deps, vanilla JS ES modules with TS typings. name: match-sorter
reason: Verified on the live repo: MIT license (confirmed), actively maintained (v8.3.0 released 2026-04-15, 129 commits, 4.1k stars) — not stale. The candidate's "zero deps" claim is FALSE: package.json declares two runtime deps, @babel/runtime (^7.23.8) and remove-accents (0.5.0), and src/index.ts literally imports remove-accents. It's TypeScript (99.7%) with a real build step (kcd-scripts bundle producing dist cjs/esm), so the "inline a copy for zero-build" pitch is misleading — the TS source isn't browser-runnable as-is and drags in remove-accents. It has NO frontend/UI; it's a headless pure-function array sorter, so there's no interaction design to reuse, only the algorithm. That algorithm is, however, an excellent fit for our command-bar/result-list ranking: a deterministic prefix-first ladder (CASE_SENSITIVE_EQUAL > EQUAL > STARTS_WITH > WORD_STARTS_WITH > CONTAINS > ACRONYM > fuzzy MATCHES) with a getClosenessRanking fractional tiebreak, nested-key extraction, and threshold options. Best used by porting the ~500-line single-file core to a vanilla-JS module and dropping remove-accents (inline a diacritics stripper or use String.normalize NFD). Hence adapt, not adopt: legally trivial under MIT, technically not a zero-dep no-build drop-in.
key_files_or_ideas: src/index.ts (single ~500-line core). Key ideas to port: getMatchRanking ladder (CASE_SENSITIVE_EQUAL=7, EQUAL=6, STARTS_WITH=5, WORD_STARTS_WITH=4, CONTAINS=3, ACRONYM=2, MATCHES=1, NO_MATCH=0); getClosenessRanking fuzzy closeness score (1..2) rewarding tight char clusters; nested `keys` accessor extraction; baseSort + threshold options. Drop remove-accents (replace with String.prototype.normalize('NFD') strip or keepDiacritics). Pairs naturally with an entity+function grammar because prefix/word-start tiers rank predictably.

name: d3-force
reason: VERIFIED. License: ISC (LICENSE file "Copyright 2010-2021 Mike Bostock", confirmed on npm) — permissive, adoptable, only requires keeping the notice. It is a pure computation module: computes node x/y via velocity-Verlet only, NO rendering/frontend/UI code — so there is no "frontend code" or "interaction design" to adapt; you write all canvas/SVG drawing, drag, zoom, and hit-testing yourself. This suits the hand-rolled skin system (max control) but means the value is the layout math, not any reusable UI. UMD build dist/d3-force.min.js (~8.3KB min) exists, exposes window.d3, no build step needed — this half of the pitch holds. CRITICAL CORRECTION to the candidate's "zero deps, one <script>" claim: it is FALSE. d3-force declares 3 runtime deps (d3-quadtree, d3-dispatch, d3-timer) and the UMD build does NOT bundle them — the browser-global branch reads them off window.d3 (factory receives n.d3,n.d3,n.d3), so a lone <script src=d3-force.min.js> throws (d3.quadtree/dispatch/timer undefined). You must vendor 4 files, not 1 (or pull the ~280KB full d3 bundle). Real footprint if vendored: d3-quadtree 3.0.1 (5.3KB) + d3-dispatch 3.0.1 (1.9KB) + d3-timer 3.0.1 (1.9KB) + d3-force 3.0.0 (8.3KB) = ~17.4KB min across 4 files, all ISC, all vanilla UMD, all zero-build. Still fully compatible with the no-npm/no-build ethos. Maturity: frozen at v3.0.0 since Jun 5 2021 — canonical and stable, not actively evolving (couldn't get exact last-commit date; GitHub API rate-limited unauthenticated), but the force API is settled and this is not a risk. Verdict adopt stands: drops in with no build step under a permissive license; the only real correction is 4 tiny vendored files instead of 1, and the honest expectation that you build all the rendering/interaction yourself.

Usage: d3.forceSimulation(nodes).force('link', d3.forceLink(links).id(d=>d.id)).force('charge', d3.forceManyBody()).force('center', d3.forceCenter(w/2,h/2)).force('collide', d3.forceCollide(r)); then on('tick', render) where render loops nodes reading node.x/node.y and draws onto your existing canvas/SVG pane. It only mutates x/y/vx/vy — the network-graph pane's drawing, drag (fx/fy pinning), zoom/pan, and node hit-testing are all yours to implement. Do NOT rely on the marketed single-<script> global; test that d3.forceSimulation exists before use.

reason: Verified against the real repo/CDN, not marketing. License is genuinely MIT (confirmed from the actual /LICENCE file: "MIT License, Copyright Contributors to the cosmos.gl project"). (4) Scope is narrow: it does nothing for the command bar, fuzzy search, entity pages, or pane tiling — only the graph.
key_files_or_ideas: dist/vivagraph.min.js (62.4 KB, self-contained, global `Viva` namespace, drop-in via <script>, no build); WebGL + SVG renderers with pluggable ngraph force layouts for the network-graph pane; API pattern Viva.Graph.graph() -> Viva.Graph.View.renderer(graph, {graphics, layout}).run(). Underlying ngraph.* modules (ngraph.graph, ngraph.forcelayout, ngraph.events) are the cherry-pickable pieces if a lighter custom renderer is preferred over the whole bundle.

name: ngraph.forcelayout / ngraph.graph (anvaka)
reason: Verified against actual repos. LICENSE CORRECTION: candidate claimed MIT, but both modules are BSD-3-Clause (package.json + LICENSE file confirmed) — still permissive and vendor-safe, but requires retaining copyright/disclaimer notice. No-build usability CONFIRMED: prebuilt UMD bundles ship on CDN exposing globals (ngraphCreate2dLayout, createGraph); the browserify+terser build is only for the maintainer, consumers drop in the dist file. Measured real sizes from jsdelivr: forcelayout 2D self-contained build 23.2 KB min, full N-D 36.5 KB min, ngraph.graph UMD 4.5 KB — deps (ngraph.events/merge/random) are inlined into the bundle so it is effectively zero-runtime-dep as a drop-in. Maintained (last commit Mar 19 2026) but maintenance-only lately; stable/mature. Layout-decoupled-from-rendering CONFIRMED: it is a pure force-sim engine outputting node positions, you render with your own canvas/SVG. SCOPE CAVEAT: it provides NO frontend UI or interaction design (no command bar, panes, entity pages, or fuzzy search) — it is solely the graph-layout primitive for the one network-graph pane. Adopt-grade for that subsystem specifically: vendor ngraph.forcelayout2d.min.js + ngraph.graph UMD, no npm/build needed.
license_confirmed: BSD-3-Clause (candidate's claimed MIT is incorrect; both ngraph.forcelayout and ngraph.graph are BSD-3-Clause per package.json and LICENSE file — permissive, adoption/vendoring OK with attribution + disclaimer retained)
key_files_or_ideas: Vendor prebuilt UMD bundles directly into the static SPA (no build step): (1) https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/ngraph.forcelayout/dist/ngraph.forcelayout2d.min.js — 23.2 KB min, self-contained, exposes global ngraphCreate2dLayout (use the 2D build, not the 36.5 KB N-D build); (2) https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/ngraph.graph/dist/ngraph.graph.umd.js — 4.5 KB, exposes global createGraph. Pattern: build a ngraph.graph of vc-brain entities (persons/forums/evidence/briefs) + edges, run forcelayout to get {x,y} positions, then draw nodes/edges with your own canvas/SVG code — keeps the network-graph pane tiny and matches the reimplement-if-small / zero-dep philosophy. Provides ONLY layout math; contributes nothing to the terminal command-bar/pane/search UI.

name: WebCola (cola.js)
reason: MIT license verified (package.json + GitHub SPDX). Ships a prebuilt self-contained UMD bundle at WebCola/cola.min.js (~80KB min; ~765KB unminified) that exposes a global `cola` via a plain <script> tag — inspected the actual minified file: UMD wrapper falls back to window.cola, and its 4 d3 sub-module deps (d3-dispatch/drag/timer/shape) are bundled INSIDE the file, so no npm, no build step, no separate D3 needed. It is a constraint-based LAYOUT engine, not a renderer: you compute node positions (alignment, non-overlap, flow/hierarchy direction, grouping) and render to SVG yourself with cola.Layout ticked manually — a perfect match for the zero-dependency vanilla-JS SPA that renders its own SVG. This yields structured, readable tiered layouts instead of a force hairball, which is exactly what a Bloomberg-style entity network wants. Verdict adopt rather than adapt because you include it as-is, not port its code. Caveats: standalone (non-D3) Layout usage is under-documented so expect a thin integration/render layer; constraint solving is heavier than plain force so best for curated graphs of dozens-to-low-hundreds of nodes (lazy-load the 80KB on the network pane); activity is mature/low-churn (core is ~2018-era v3.x; recent commits are only README fixes and Dependabot bumps; npm latest 3.4.0), not actively developed but stable and not archived.
key_files_or_ideas: Prebuilt drop-in file: WebCola/cola.min.js (~80KB, exposes global `cola` via UMD; cola.js ~765KB unminified). Core API: `cola.Layout` used standalone — call layout.nodes()/links()/constraints(), start(), and read each node's .x/.y to draw your own SVG circles/lines (no D3 needed for rendering). Reusable design ideas for the entity graph: constraint types that give terminal-grade readable layouts — alignment constraints (tiered rows/columns), non-overlap (avoidOverlaps), flow/directed-edge constraints (top-down or left-right hierarchy), and grouping (bounded clusters for e.g. forums/cohorts). `cola.powerGraph` for module/cluster abstraction. CDN for quick eval: https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/webcola@3.4.0/WebCola/cola.min.js. npm: webcola@3.4.0. Repo: https://github.com/tgdwyer/WebCola (MIT).
license_confirmed: MIT — verified in package.json ("license":"MIT") and via GitHub API SPDX classification. Repo is public, not archived/disabled.

name: Springy
reason: Verified against the live repo. MIT license (real LICENSE file, Dennis Hotson 2010) — vendorable. No build step; plain script-tag JS. Last commit March 6, 2018 — genuinely dormant/legacy (~8 yrs stale), 1.9k stars. Repo splits into two parts: springy.js (~650 lines) is a self-contained, ZERO-dependency UMD core (Graph/Node/Edge + loadJSON, 2D Vector math, Layout.ForceDirected using Coulomb+Hooke+damping with energy convergence, Renderer loop) — this is the adaptable asset for a custom network-graph pane. BUT the shipped renderer/interaction file springyui.js (~400 lines), which the candidate summary sells as a 'small canvas renderer helper', is actually a jQuery PLUGIN (jQuery.fn.springy, jQuery mouse/window event binding) and is NOT zero-dep compatible; its canvas drawing (arrowheads, labels) is reusable only as reference after stripping jQuery. No command bar / fuzzy search / tiling to draw on — only the physics is relevant. So: port the physics core, reimplement rendering in vanilla JS. 'adapt', not 'adopt', because the frontend layer as-shipped needs jQuery.
key_files_or_ideas: springy.js (~650 lines, UMD, zero external deps): Graph/Node/Edge model with loadJSON, Vector 2D math, Layout.ForceDirected (Coulomb repulsion + Hooke spring + damping + energy-based convergence), Renderer loop abstraction — port/vendor this for the network-graph pane. springyui.js (~400 lines, jQuery-coupled) — reference only for canvas drawing: node/edge rendering, arrowhead geometry, edge/node label placement, drag-to-move; must be rewritten with addEventListener to fit the zero-dependency vanilla-JS SPA.

name: arbor.js
reason: Verified against the real repo. MIT license (confirmed in source header, Copyright 2012 Samizdat Drafting Co.) so legally clean. But last commit is May 28, 2012 — abandoned ~14 years, no releases. It requires jQuery (IIFE wrapped as `})(this.jQuery)`), which violates the zero-dependency rule as-shipped. Notably the jQuery coupling is shallow/utility-only in core (just $.each x36, $.extend x3, $.isArray x5, $.inArray x2 — all trivially swappable for forEach/Object.assign/Array.isArray/indexOf), so it is technically portable. The genuinely valuable idea is verified: physics runs in a web worker (`new Worker(arbor_path()+"arbor.js")`), keeping the main thread responsive. However, arbor is a LAYOUT ENGINE ONLY — by design it draws nothing (leaves canvas/SVG/HTML rendering to you), so it provides none of the actual graph pane, hit-testing, or interaction we need. For our entity network-graph pane, adopting is impossible (jQuery + dead since 2012) and porting the 2012 physics yields only layout — worse value than a modern zero-dep force sim. Keep it as a design reference for the worker-offloaded-layout pattern, exactly as the candidate summary states. Tiny (lib 28KB minified; ~48KB source across 4 files), no build step required to use.
key_files_or_ideas: Design pattern to study, not code to lift: offload force-directed layout to a Web Worker so the terminal UI stays responsive (arbor loads itself as its own worker via `new Worker(arbor_path()+"arbor.js")`). Reference files: src/physics/physics.js (12KB, force sim), src/physics/system.js (22KB, graph model + worker bridge), src/physics/worker.js. Note it renders nothing — all drawing/interaction for our entity graph pane must be written by us. jQuery coupling is only utility functions ($.each/$.extend/$.isArray/$.inArray), so the pattern translates cleanly to vanilla if we ever port it.

name: Reagraph
reason: Verified: Apache-2.0 license (confirmed via repo badge + package.json — candidate had this "unverified"), actively maintained (npm v4.32.0 published ~21 days ago, ~1.1k stars, TS). But it is a hard architectural mismatch. package.json confirms peer deps react/react-dom (>=16) plus runtime deps three@^0.184.0, @react-three/fiber@^9.6.1, @react-three/drei, @react-spring/three, and several d3-* packages — it IS the full react-three-fiber stack. Build outputs (main/module/browser) all point to a single ESM ./dist/index.js with bare-specifier imports; there is no UMD/IIFE/standalone bundle, so it cannot be dropped into a no-build vanilla page. Using it means abandoning the zero-dep no-framework stack (React + react-dom + three + r3f + drei + a bundler). Not portable as code either: rendering is declarative react-three-fiber, so logic can't be lifted to vanilla without a rewrite. Its design is a generic force-directed/clustered WebGL graph offering no terminal-specific interaction pattern; for a vanilla network-graph pane, sigma.js/cytoscape.js are far more relevant references. Reconsider only if the frontend migrates to React.
key_files_or_ideas: Repo: github.com/reaviz/reagraph. package.json confirms React peer deps + three@^0.184.0 + @react-three/fiber@^9.6.1 + @react-three/drei + @react-spring/three + d3-array/d3-force-3d/d3-hierarchy/d3-scale. Only build artifact is ESM ./dist/index.js (main/module/browser all point there); no standalone/vanilla bundle. Not usable without React and a bundler. Only conceptual takeaway: clustering + 2D/3D layout modes for a graph, which are better sourced from vanilla-native sigma.js/cytoscape.js.

name: awesome-command-palette
reason: Verified against the live repo: it is a pure curated markdown link-list, exactly three files (LICENSE, README.md, screenshot.png) with ZERO code — no frontend, no build, no snippets to port. So it cannot be 'adopt' (nothing to install) or 'adapt' (no code to translate). Its sole value is as a discovery/research index: mine the README to study command-palette interaction patterns for our 'entity + function' command bar and to find leaner vanilla-friendly implementations elsewhere (it even points to astro-command-palette, '0 dependencies'). License verified MIT, Copyright (c) 2021 Stefan Judis — but that MIT covers the list text only; every linked project carries its own license, so any code you eventually reuse must be re-vetted. Repo is lightly maintained (last updated ~Aug 2025, ~311 stars). Downgraded from any code-reuse verdict to 'reference' because there is no reusable artifact here — only a well-organized set of pointers.
license_confirmed: MIT — verified from raw LICENSE file, Copyright (c) 2021 Stefan Judis. Covers list content only; each linked project has its own license.
key_files_or_ideas: README.md is the entire useful surface: a categorized index of command-palette implementations (sites, browser extensions, desktop apps, OS launchers, JS/React libraries, terminal tools). For our zero-dep vanilla SPA, use it to (1) study palette interaction/grammar patterns for the AAPL-Equity-DES-style command bar, and (2) discover leaner candidates to evaluate separately — notably astro-command-palette ('0 dependencies'); most listed libs (kbar, etc.) are React and not directly usable. No files to copy from this repo itself.
